The patient's electrical axis is approximately **+37°**, which is normal. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ECG Interval Abnormalities (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following matrix is **board-exam gold**. Commit it to memory. | Interval | Normal Duration | Prolonged → Indicates | Shortened → Indicates | |---|---|---|---| | **PR Interval** | 120 – 200 ms (3–5 small boxes) | First-degree AV block | Pre-excitation (WPW syndrome), junctional rhythm | | **QRS Duration** | < 120 ms (< 3 small boxes) | Bundle branch block, ventricular rhythm, hyperkalemia | — (rarely clinically significant) | | **QT Interval** | 350 – 440 ms (rate-corrected: QTc < 440 ms in men, < 460 ms in women) | Hypocalcemia, drug toxicity (quinidine, sotalol), congenital long-QT → **risk of Torsades de Pointes** | Hypercalcemia, digitalis effect | | **ST Segment** | Isoelectric | Ischemia (elevated → STEMI; depressed → subendocardial ischemia) | Early repolarization (often benign) | ## 3.5 Numerical Practice: Heart Rate & Axis ### Problem 1 A patient's ECG shows an R-R interval of 4 big boxes. Compute the heart rate. **Solution:** $$N_{big} = 4 \text{ big boxes} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ small boxes}$$ Using the 1500 rule: $$\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$ Equivalently, using the 300 rule: $$\text{HR} = \frac{300}{4} = 75 \text{ bpm} \quad \checkmark$$ ### Problem 2 Net QRS deflection: Lead I = +4 mm, aVF = –6 mm. Find the axis and interpret. **Solution:** $$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-6}{+4}\right) = \arctan(-1.5) = -56.3^\circ$$ Since $QRS_I > 0$ and $QRS_{aVF} < 0$, the axis lies in the **left superior quadrant** (0° to –90°). This is **Left Axis Deviation (LAD)**, which can indicate left anterior fascicular block, left ventricular hypertrophy, or inferior MI. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cardiac Conduction System — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD SAN["SA Node
Rate 70 to 100 bpm
Pacemaker 1"] ATR["Atrial Internodal Tracts
Bachmann Wenckebach Thorel"] AVN["AV Node
Rate 40 to 60 bpm
Delay 100 ms"] BH["Bundle of His
Only electrical bridge"] LBB["Left Bundle Branch"] RBB["Right Bundle Branch"] PFK["Purkinje Fiber Network
Rate 20 to 40 bpm"] VENT["Ventricular Myocardium
Contractile tissue"] SAN --> ATR ATR --> AVN AVN --> BH BH --> LBB BH --> RBB LBB --> PFK RBB --> PFK PFK --> VENT SAN -.Pacemaker Hierarchy.-> AVN AVN -.Pacemaker Hierarchy.-> PFK ``` > [!NOTE] > The **dashed arrows** represent the *escape pacemaker hierarchy* — if the SA node fails, the AV node takes over; if AV fails, the Purkinje network generates a slow idioventricular rhythm. ## 4.2 ECG Waveform — Component Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P["P Wave
Atrial Depolarization
Duration less than 120 ms"] PR["PR Segment
AV Nodal Delay
50 to 120 ms"] PRI["PR Interval
Start P to Start QRS
120 to 200 ms"] Q["Q Wave
Septal Depolarization"] R["R Wave
Main Ventricular Depol"] S["S Wave
Late Ventricular Depol"] QRS["QRS Complex
Total Vent Depol
less than 120 ms"] ST["ST Segment
Phase 2 Plateau
80 to 120 ms"] T["T Wave
Ventricular Repol
Asymmetric"] QTI["QT Interval
Total Ventricular Systole
350 to 440 ms"] U["U Wave
Purkinje Repol
Optional"] P --> PR PR --> Q Q --> R R --> S Q --> QRS R --> QRS S --> QRS QRS --> ST ST --> T T --> U P --> PRI Q --> PRI QRS --> QTI T --> QTI ``` ## 4.3 Einthoven's Triangle — Frontal Plane Lead Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph TRIANGLE["Einthoven Triangle — Frontal Plane"] RA["RA Electrode
Right Arm
Negative for I and II"] LA["LA Electrode
Left Arm
Positive for I and III"] LL["LL Electrode
Left Leg
Positive for II and III"] end HEART["Cardiac Dipole
at Triangle Centroid"] RA -.Lead III negative.-> LA LA -.Lead I positive.-> RA LA -.Lead III positive.-> LL LL -.Lead II positive.-> RA RA -.Lead II negative.-> LL HEART --- RA HEART --- LA HEART --- LL LI["Lead I axis 0 degrees"] LII["Lead II axis 60 degrees"] LIII["Lead III axis 120 degrees"] AVR["aVR axis minus 150 degrees"] AVL["aVL axis minus 30 degrees"] AVF["aVF axis plus 90 degrees"] LI ~~~ LII LII ~~~ LIII LIII ~~~ AVR AVR ~~~ AVL AVL ~~~ AVF ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The three augmented leads ($aV_R$, $aV_L$, $aV_F$) are placed at the **midpoints of the triangle sides**, not at the vertices, which is why they are called *augmented* unipolar leads (Goldberger's modification gives a 50% larger signal than the original Wilson unipolar leads). ## 4.4 Block Diagram of an ECG Machine ```mermaid flowchart LR BODY["Patient Body Surface
10 to 12 Electrodes"] ELEC["Electrode Interface
Ag AgCl with Electrode Gel
Skin Preparation"] PROT["Patient Protection
Isolation Amplifier
Defibrillator Protection"] IA["Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 1000
CMRR greater than 100 dB"] BPF["Band Pass Filter
0.05 Hz to 150 Hz"] NF["Notch Filter
50 Hz or 60 Hz Rejection"] AGC["Automatic Gain Control"] ADC["Analog to Digital Converter
Sampling Rate 500 Hz minimum"] DSP["Digital Signal Processing
Pan Tompkins R peak Detection"] DISP["Display and Storage
LCD Monitor and Memory"] BODY --> ELEC ELEC --> PROT PROT --> IA IA --> BPF BPF --> NF NF --> AGC AGC --> ADC ADC --> DSP DSP --> DISP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering rationale for each block:** > - **Ag/AgCl electrodes** — non-polarizable, stable half-cell potential (~+0.22 V). > - **Instrumentation amplifier** — high CMRR rejects common-mode 50/60 Hz interference picked up by the body. > - **0.05 Hz HPF** — blocks DC electrode offset (which can be hundreds of mV). > - **150 Hz LPF** — limits thermal noise and aliasing. > - **Notch filter** — surgical removal of residual powerline interference. > - **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** — derivative → square → moving-window integration → adaptive thresholding; the gold-standard QRS detector. ## 4.5 Precordial Lead Placement Map ```mermaid flowchart TD CHEST["Anterior Chest Wall"] V1["V1 4th ICS Right Sternal Border"] V2["V2 4th ICS Left Sternal Border"] V4["V4 5th ICS Mid Clavicular Line Apex"] V3["V3 Midway V2 to V4"] V5["V5 Anterior Axillary Line at V4 Level"] V6["V6 Mid Axillary Line at V4 Level"] CHEST --> V1 CHEST --> V2 CHEST --> V3 CHEST --> V4 CHEST --> V5 CHEST --> V6 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Define electrocardiogram. List the standard ECG leads and explain Einthoven's triangle in brief.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The electrocardiogram (ECG) is a graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart, obtained by placing electrodes on the body surface and amplifying the small bioelectric potentials (in the millivolt range) generated by the depolarization and repolarization of the cardiac muscle. **Standard Leads (1 mark):** There are 12 standard ECG leads: - 3 Bipolar limb leads: Lead I, Lead II, Lead III - 3 Augmented unipolar limb leads: aVR, aVL, aVF - 6 Precordial (chest) unipolar leads: V1 to V6 **Einthoven's Triangle (1 mark):** Proposed by Willem Einthoven in 1903, it is an equilateral triangle with the heart at the centroid, formed by connecting the three limb electrodes (Right Arm, Left Arm, Left Leg). The cardiac dipole vector projected onto each side of this triangle gives the three bipolar leads. The fundamental relation is **Lead I + Lead III = Lead II**, derived from Kirchhoff's voltage law. --- ### Question A.2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: With a neat sketch, explain the normal ECG waveform and label all waves, complexes, and intervals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The normal ECG waveform consists of the following sequential components (sketch for 2 marks, labeling 1 mark): 1. **P-wave:** Small, rounded, positive in most leads. Represents **atrial depolarization**. Duration: < 120 ms. 2. **PR segment:** Isoelectric line representing **AV nodal conduction delay**. 3. **QRS complex:** Sharp, large deflection representing **ventricular depolarization**. Composed of Q (initial downward), R (large upward), S (downward after R). Duration: < 120 ms. 4. **ST segment:** Isoelectric segment representing the **plateau phase (Phase 2)** of ventricular action potential — all ventricular cells uniformly depolarized. 5. **T-wave:** Asymmetric, rounded wave representing **ventricular repolarization**. 6. **U-wave:** Small, low-amplitude wave (sometimes invisible) — **Purkinje fiber repolarization**. 7. **PR interval:** 120–200 ms (start of P to start of QRS). 8. **QT interval:** 350–440 ms (start of QRS to end of T). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > *In KTU ESE, Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically offer an internal choice. The two questions below represent alternative choices. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.* --- ### Question B.1 (14 Marks) — OPTION A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2, Set A]** #### (a) Explain the electrical activity of the heart in detail. Describe the cardiac conduction system, the cardiac action potential, and the pacemaker hierarchy. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Cardiac Action Potential (3 marks):** The cardiac muscle cell, unlike a nerve cell, exhibits a characteristic **action potential with a prolonged plateau phase**, lasting 200–300 ms. The phases are: - **Phase 4 – Resting potential (–90 mV):** Maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle), giving a net negative intracellular potential. - **Phase 0 – Rapid depolarization:** Voltage-gated fast Na⁺ channels open, Na⁺ rushes in, membrane potential rises to +20 to +30 mV. - **Phase 1 – Initial repolarization:** Brief K⁺ efflux, transient dip. - **Phase 2 – Plateau phase:** Slow Ca²⁺ influx through L-type calcium channels balances K⁺ efflux. This plateau is **unique to cardiac muscle** and prevents tetanic contraction — the cell cannot be re-stimulated until repolarization is complete (long **absolute refractory period**, ~250 ms). - **Phase 3 – Repolarization:** K⁺ efflux dominates, membrane returns to –90 mV. - **Phase 4 – Resting (in non-pacemaker cells)** or **spontaneous diastolic depolarization (in pacemaker cells like SA node)**. **Cardiac Conduction System (3 marks):** The heart has a specialized conduction system that ensures coordinated, sequential contraction: 1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node:** Located in the right atrium near the SVC opening. Generates impulses at 70–100 bpm. **Primary pacemaker.** 2. **Atrial Internodal Tracts (Bachmann's, Wenckebach's, Thorel's):** Conduct the impulse from SA node to AV node and across both atria (P-wave). 3. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node:** Located in the interatrial septum near the coronary sinus opening. **Introduces a 100 ms delay** to allow complete atrial emptying before ventricular contraction. Intrinsic rate 40–60 bpm. 4. **Bundle of His:** The only electrical pathway from atria to ventricles. 5. **Right and Left Bundle Branches:** Travel down the interventricular septum toward the apex. 6. **Purkinje Fibers:** Rapidly conduct the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium (QRS complex). Intrinsic rate 20–40 bpm. 7. **Ventricular Myocardium:** Working contractile tissue. **Pacemaker Hierarchy (1 mark):** The fastest pacemaker dominates. Hierarchy (fastest to slowest): - SA Node → 70–100 bpm (dominant) - AV Node (junctional escape) → 40–60 bpm - Purkinje/Ventricular (idioventricular escape) → 20–40 bpm If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over; if both fail, ventricular cells generate a slow escape rhythm. #### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an ECG machine. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram Description (5 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation):** The ECG machine consists of the following sequential blocks: 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** 10–12 electrodes placed on limbs and chest. Ag/AgCl is preferred because it is **non-polarizable** and gives a stable half-cell potential of +0.22 V. Electrode gel minimizes skin–electrode impedance. 2. **Patient Protection Circuit:** Includes current-limiting resistors, isolation transformers, and defibrillator protection diodes to prevent microshock and macroshock to the patient. 3. **Lead Selector Network:** A switching matrix that selects which electrode pair is connected to the amplifier, allowing sequential display of all 12 leads. 4. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** A three-op-amp configuration providing: - High differential gain (~1000) - Very high CMRR (> 100 dB) to reject common-mode 50/60 Hz interference - High input impedance (> 10 MΩ) to avoid loading the body 5. **Band-Pass Filter:** - High-pass filter at 0.05 Hz blocks DC electrode offset. - Low-pass filter at 150 Hz limits high-frequency noise and prevents aliasing. 6. **Notch Filter:** A narrow band-reject filter centered at 50 Hz (India/Europe) or 60 Hz (USA) to eliminate powerline interference. 7. **Automatic Gain Control (AGC):** Adjusts gain dynamically to keep the QRS within display range. 8. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples the ECG at ≥ 500 Hz with 12-bit (or higher) resolution to faithfully capture the QRS complex (which has frequency components up to ~150 Hz). 9. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Implements the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for real-time QRS detection (derivative, squaring, moving-average integration, adaptive thresholding). 10. **Display and Storage:** LCD monitor, thermal printer, and digital memory (SD card / cloud). **Working Principle (2 marks):** The cardiac dipole generates potentials of 0.5–5 mV on the body surface. The electrodes pick up these signals; the instrumentation amplifier boosts them ~1000×; filters remove DC drift and 50 Hz noise; the ADC digitizes the waveform; the DSP detects R-peaks and computes heart rate; the cleaned waveform is displayed on the screen and optionally printed on thermal paper at 25 mm/s with 10 mm/mV calibration. --- ### Question B.2 (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set B]** #### (a) Derive Einthoven's law. Using the law, verify whether the following recorded ECG lead values are consistent: Lead I = +0.7 mV, Lead II = +1.0 mV, Lead III = +0.3 mV. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Derivation of Einthoven's Law (4 marks):** Let $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_F$ be the potentials at the Right Arm, Left Arm, and Left Foot electrodes respectively. The three bipolar limb leads are defined as: $$\text{Lead I} = V_L - V_R$$ $$\text{Lead II} = V_F - V_R$$ $$\text{Lead III} = V_F - V_L$$ Adding Lead I and Lead III algebraically: $$\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = (V_L - V_R) + (V_F - V_L)$$ The $+V_L$ from Lead I and the $-V_L$ from Lead III cancel out: $$\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = V_F - V_R$$ But $V_F - V_R$ is, by definition, Lead II. Therefore: $$\boxed{\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II}} \quad \blacksquare$$ This is known as **Einthoven's Law**, and it is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed loop formed by the three limb electrodes through the body volume conductor. **Verification of Given Values (3 marks):** Given: - Lead I = +0.7 mV - Lead II = +1.0 mV - Lead III = +0.3 mV **Check:** Lead I + Lead III = 0.7 + 0.3 = **1.0 mV** = Lead II ✓ Since the equality holds exactly, the recorded ECG lead values are **internally consistent**. There is no evidence of lead misconnection or electrode fault. The cardiac dipole vector at this instant projects onto: - Lead I axis: +0.7 mV - Lead III axis: +0.3 mV - Lead II axis: +1.0 mV This is a normal, consistent reading. --- #### (b) Explain the 12-lead ECG system. List all 12 leads, their placement, and the cardiac region each lead views. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** A **standard 12-lead ECG** is obtained from **10 electrodes** (4 limb + 6 precordial), but produces **12 different "views" (leads)** of the heart's electrical activity. **Limb Electrodes (4):** - **RA** (Right Arm) – lateral aspect of right deltoid - **LA** (Left Arm) – lateral aspect of left deltoid - **LL** (Left Leg) – medial aspect of left calf - **RL** (Right Leg) – medial aspect of right calf (ground reference) **Precordial Electrodes (6):** - V1 – 4th intercostal space, right sternal border - V2 – 4th intercostal space, left sternal border - V3 – midway between V2 and V4 - V4 – 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line - V5 – anterior axillary line, same level as V4 - V6 – mid-axillary line, same level as V4 **The 12 Leads (3 marks for table):** | # | Lead | Type | Axis (frontal plane) | Cardiac Region Viewed | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Lead I** | Bipolar | 0° | Lateral LV | | 2 | **Lead II** | Bipolar | +60° | Inferior wall (LV + RV apex) | | 3 | **Lead III** | Bipolar | +120° | Inferior wall (RV + LV) | | 4 | **aVR** | Augmented unipolar | –150° | Right atrial cavity, RV base | | 5 | **aVL** | Augmented unipolar | –30° | High lateral LV | | 6 | **aVF** | Augmented unipolar | +90° | Inferior wall (LV) | | 7 | **V1** | Precordial unipolar | — | Septal / RV | | 8 | **V2** | Precordial unipolar | — | Septal / RV | | 9 | **V3** | Precordial unipolar | — | Anterior wall (transition) | | 10 | **V4** | Precordial unipolar | — | Apex / anterior LV | | 11 | **V5** | Precordial unipolar | — | Lateral LV | | 12 | **V6** | Precordial unipolar | — | Lateral LV | **Clinical Significance (2 marks):** - **Inferior MI** → changes in Leads II, III, aVF - **Lateral MI** → changes in Leads I, aVL, V5, V6 - **Anterior MI** → changes in V1–V4 - **Septal MI** → changes in V1, V2 - **Right-sided leads (V3R, V4R)** are added for suspected RV infarction. The 12-lead ECG thus provides a comprehensive 3D-like view of the heart, with the **6 limb leads** projecting the cardiac vector onto the **frontal plane** and the **6 precordial leads** projecting onto the **horizontal (transverse) plane**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye)** > > 1. **Confusing PR interval with PR segment:** The PR *interval* (120–200 ms) starts at the **beginning of the P-wave** and ends at the **beginning of the QRS**. The PR *segment* is the isoelectric portion *between* the end of P and start of QRS. Many students write the wrong duration for one of them. > > 2. **Einthoven's Law sign errors:** Students often write Lead I + Lead II = Lead III or Lead I = Lead II + Lead III. The correct identity is **Lead I + Lead III = Lead II**. Derive it explicitly in the exam — even if you remember the result, the *derivation* carries 3–4 marks. > > 3. **Forgetting the cardiac action potential plateau:** The plateau (Phase 2) sustained by Ca²⁺ influx is the single most distinguishing feature of cardiac vs. neuronal action potentials. Examiners specifically look for this. > > 4. **Confusing "lead" with "electrode":** A *lead* is a *viewpoint* (a voltage difference between two electrodes). An *electrode* is a physical sensor. 10 electrodes → 12 leads. This distinction is worth 1 mark. > > 5. **Wrong paper speed assumption:** The KTU standard is **25 mm/s** and **10 mm/mV**. Using 50 mm/s in your calculations will halve all intervals and double the heart rate. > > 6. **Not labelling units:** Writing "1500/N" without saying "where N = number of small boxes in R-R interval" loses a mark. > > 7. **Skipping the block diagram:** In Question B.1(b), the **block diagram carries 5 of 7 marks**. A clear, well-labelled diagram with arrows is non-negotiable. Even if your text is weak, a good diagram can pull your score up. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Recap** - ✅ The **ECG** is the surface recording of the **summed cardiac dipole activity**, normally 0.5–5 mV in amplitude. - ✅ The heart's conduction pathway: **SA node → Atrial tracts → AV node → Bundle of His → Bundle branches → Purkinje fibers → Ventricular myocardium**. - ✅ Pacemaker hierarchy: **SA (70–100) > AV (40–60) > Purkinje (20–40) bpm**. - ✅ Cardiac action potential has a **plateau phase (Phase 2)** due to **Ca²⁺ influx** — this prevents tetanus. - ✅ **P-wave** = atrial depolarization. **QRS** = ventricular depolarization. **T-wave** = ventricular repolarization. **U-wave** = Purkinje repolarization. - ✅ **PR interval** = 120–200 ms. **QRS** < 120 ms. **QT interval** = 350–440 ms. - ✅ **12 standard leads** = 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented unipolar (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6). - ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** Lead I + Lead III = Lead II (from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law). - ✅ **Einthoven's Triangle:** Equilateral triangle with heart at centroid; formed by RA, LA, LL electrodes. - ✅ **Augmented leads (Goldberger):** $aV_R$, $aV_L$, $aV_F$ — reference is the average of the other two limb electrodes. - ✅ **Precordial leads V1–V6** are unipolar with the **Wilson Central Terminal** as reference. - ✅ **Standard ECG paper:** **25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV**. 1 small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s. 1 big box = 5 mm = 0.20 s. - ✅ **Heart rate (regular rhythm):** **1500 / (number of small boxes in R-R interval)** bpm — or **300 / (big boxes in R-R interval)** bpm. - ✅ **Mean electrical axis (normal):** –30° to +90°; computed from net QRS in Lead I and aVF using arctangent. - ✅ **ECG machine blocks:** Electrode → Protection → Lead selector → Instrumentation amp → BPF → Notch filter → AGC → ADC → DSP → Display. - ✅ **Instrumentation amplifier** must have CMRR > 100 dB to reject 50/60 Hz common-mode noise. - ✅ **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the standard DSP technique for real-time QRS detection. - ✅ **Clinical rule of infarct localization:** II, III, aVF → inferior; I, aVL, V5, V6 → lateral; V1–V4 → anterior. - ✅ **Atrial repolarization (Ta-wave)** is normally hidden inside the QRS complex. - ✅ **AV nodal delay (~100 ms)** allows complete ventricular filling before systole — loss of this delay is **fatal**. - ✅ The heart cannot be tetanized because of its **long absolute refractory period (~250 ms)**, a direct consequence of the **Phase 2 plateau**. > **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | OEBMT721 | Module 2 | ECG**

topics.title: ECG Lead Systems, ECG Machine Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG Lead Systems, ECG Machine Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an ECG Lead System?

An **ECG Lead System** is a standardized configuration of surface electrodes placed on the human body that measures the electrical activity of the heart from specific anatomical viewpoints, producing the diagnostic waveform known as the electrocardiogram (ECG). Each "lead" does not represent a single wire but rather a *virtual voltage vector* representing the difference in electrical potential between two (or more) electrode positions on the body surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A "lead" is a *view* of the heart's electrical activity, not a physical wire. The 12-lead ECG system, therefore, provides **12 different electrical viewpoints** of the same cardiac event using only **10 physical electrodes**.

## 1.2 The 12-Lead ECG System — Standard Configuration

The 12-lead ECG comprises:

| Lead Type | Number | Name | Configuration |
|-----------|--------|------|---------------|
| Bipolar Limb Leads | 3 | Lead I, Lead II, Lead III | Einthoven's Triangle |
| Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads | 3 | aVR, aVL, aVF | Goldberger's Configuration |
| Precordial (Chest) Leads | 6 | V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 | Wilson's Central Terminal |
| **TOTAL LEADS** | **12** | 12 views | 10 electrodes used |

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining 4 electrodes (2 legs, 1 ground/reference, plus the chest electrode counted in the 6 precordials) come from a total of **10 physical electrodes** placed on the patient.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Heart as an Orchestra"

Imagine the heart as a **large orchestra** playing a symphony. The ECG machine is a recording studio, and the electrodes are microphones placed around the concert hall:

- **Lead I (Left microphone)** listens from the left side of the hall.
- **Lead II (Front microphone)** listens from the front-center.
- **V1–V6 (Roving microphones)** are positioned in a curved arc directly above the audience, near the heart's surface.

Each microphone captures the *same music* (the same depolarization wavefront), but from a different angle. By combining all 12 microphone recordings, a sound engineer (the cardiologist) can reconstruct a complete 3D picture of where the music is loudest, when it changes tempo (arrhythmia), and which section is out of tune (ischemia/infarction). The 12-lead system gives the physician a **spatial map of cardiac electrical activity**.

## 1.4 What is an ECG Machine?

An **ECG Machine (Electrocardiograph)** is a bioelectrical signal acquisition system designed to detect, amplify, filter, record, and display the micro-volt-level cardiac potentials picked up by the body-surface electrodes. Modern ECG machines digitize the signal and often include automated interpretation algorithms.

**Key ECG Signal Characteristics (must be remembered):**

- **Frequency range:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (clinical ECG: 0.5 Hz to 50 Hz)
- **Amplitude range:** **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (typical QRS peak ≈ **1 mV**)
- **Electrode-skin impedance:** ideally < **5 kΩ**
- **Standard paper speed:** **25 mm/s**
- **Vertical calibration:** **10 mm/mV**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Groups of Leads in Detail

### A. Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven, 1913)

These measure the potential difference between **two** limb electrodes:

$$
V_{I} = \phi_{L} - \phi_{R}
$$

$$
V_{II} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R}
$$

$$
V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{L}
$$

Where:
- $\phi_{R}$ = Right arm potential
- $\phi_{L}$ = Left arm potential
- $\phi_{F}$ = Left leg (Foot) potential

The three limb electrodes (R, L, F) form **Einthoven's Triangle** — an equilateral triangle centered on the heart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Einthoven's Law states that at any instant:
> $$V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$$
> This is a fundamental consistency check used in KTU numerical problems and exam questions.

### B. Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger, 1942)

These use a **single exploring electrode** referenced against a *modified* central terminal formed by the other two limb electrodes connected through a resistive network.

$$
V_{aVR} = \phi_{R} - \frac{\phi_{L} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVL} = \phi_{L} - \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVF} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R} + \phi_{L}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The augmented leads use a central reference that is the **average of only two limb electrodes** (not all three as in Wilson's CT). The augmentation factor of **1.5** is built into the Goldberger network — this is why they are called "augmented."

### C. Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson, 1944)

Six chest electrodes placed at specific intercostal spaces:

| Lead | Position |
|------|----------|
| V1 | 4th ICS, right sternal border |
| V2 | 4th ICS, left sternal border |
| V3 | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| V4 | 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line |
| V5 | Same horizontal level as V4, anterior axillary line |
| V6 | Same horizontal level as V4, mid-axillary line |

These use **Wilson's Central Terminal (WCT)** as reference:

$$
V_{WCT} = \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}}{3}
$$

And:

$$
V_{V_n} = \phi_{V_n} - V_{WCT}
$$

## 2.2 The 12-Lead Viewpoint Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip — Cardiac Regions Mapped by Leads:**
> - **Inferior wall of heart** → Leads II, III, aVF
> - **Lateral wall** → Leads I, aVL, V5, V6
> - **Septal wall** → Leads V1, V2
> - **Anterior wall** → Leads V3, V4
> - **Right atrium / Right ventricle** → V1, V2, aVR

## 2.3 ECG Machine — Functional Block Analysis

A modern ECG machine follows this signal-chain architecture:

**Electrode → Lead Selector → Preamplifier → Main Amplifier → Filters (HPF + LPF + Notch) → ADC → Microcontroller → Display/Printer**

### 2.3.1 Detailed Block-by-Block Theory

**1. Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)**
- Convert ionic skin current → electronic current
- Half-cell potential ≈ **+0.223 V** for Ag/AgCl
- Use electrode gel to reduce skin-electrode impedance

**2. Lead Selector Network**
- A resistor network (Wilson's network: three 5 kΩ resistors) that combines the limb electrode potentials to form reference terminals
- Allows the user to select any of the 12 leads via multiplexer

**3. Preamplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier)**
- High **CMRR > 100 dB** to reject 50/60 Hz interference and common-mode noise
- Gain ≈ **×10 to ×100**
- High input impedance (> **10 MΩ**) to prevent signal loading

**4. Main Amplifier**
- Provides the bulk of the gain (total system gain ≈ **1000** to **5000**)
- Output is calibrated to **1 cm/mV** on the display

**5. Filters**
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Time constant **τ = 3.2 s** → $f_c = 1/({2\pi\tau}) \approx$ **0.05 Hz** (removes DC baseline drift)
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Cutoff at **150 Hz** (removes high-frequency muscle noise)
- **Notch Filter:** Centered at **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (mains line interference)

**6. Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**
- Sampling rate ≥ **500 samples/second** per channel (per AAMI/ANSI standards)
- Resolution: typically **12-bit** to **16-bit**
- Quantization step: $q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$

**7. Microcontroller / DSP**
- Performs R-peak detection, HR calculation, optional ST-segment analysis
- Drives the LCD/thermal printer

**8. Display / Recorder**
- Thermal array printer at **25 mm/s** paper speed
- **1 mV = 10 mm** vertical deflection

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{I} = \phi_{L} - \phi_{R}$ | Lead I potential | mV |
| 2 | $V_{II} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R}$ | Lead II potential | mV |
| 3 | $V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{L}$ | Lead III potential | mV |
| 4 | $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ | **Einthoven's Law** | mV |
| 5 | $V_{aVR} = \phi_{R} - (\phi_{L} + \phi_{F})/2$ | Augmented VR | mV |
| 6 | $V_{aVL} = \phi_{L} - (\phi_{R} + \phi_{F})/2$ | Augmented VL | mV |
| 7 | $V_{aVF} = \phi_{F} - (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L})/2$ | Augmented VF | mV |
| 8 | $V_{WCT} = (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F})/3$ | Wilson Central Terminal | mV |
| 9 | $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$ | Augmented lead sum law | mV |
| 10 | $f_c(H) = {1}/{({2\pi \cdot 3.2})} \approx 0.05\ \text{Hz}$ | ECG HPF cutoff | Hz |
| 11 | $G_{total} = G_{pre} \times G_{main}$ | Total amplifier gain | dimensionless |
| 12 | $q = V_{FS}/2^n$ | ADC quantization step | V/LSB |
| 13 | $HR = {1500}/{RR\ \text{interval (in mm)}}$ | Heart rate (paper method) | bpm |
| 14 | $CMRR = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$ | Common-mode rejection | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> Note carefully: $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$ is the *augmented* equivalent of Einthoven's law and is a **favourite KTU short-answer question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Diagnosis:** Detection of myocardial infarction (MI), arrhythmias, conduction blocks, electrolyte imbalances
- **Holter Monitoring:** Portable 12-lead variants for 24–48 hour ambulatory recording
- **Cardiac Telemetry:** Wireless 12-lead systems in ambulances
- **AI-Based Auto-Interpretation:** Modern machines use deep learning to flag ST-elevation MI (STEMI) within seconds
- **Defibrillator Synchronization:** The R-wave detected by the ECG machine triggers synchronized cardioversion

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Einthoven's Law (KTU Favourite)

**Goal:** Show that $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Lead I** (potential difference between Left arm and Right arm):

$$
V_{I} = \phi_{L} - \phi_{R}
$$

This expresses Lead I as the voltage seen by the left-arm electrode minus the voltage seen by the right-arm electrode. Each electrode acts as a probe measuring the local bioelectric potential on the skin surface.

**Step 2 — Write the definition of Lead III** (potential difference between Left leg and Left arm):

$$
V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{L}
$$

Lead III is the voltage at the left leg (foot) minus the voltage at the left arm. It captures the heart's electrical activity from a different orientation.

**Step 3 — Add Leads I and III term by term:**

$$
V_{I} + V_{III} = (\phi_{L} - \phi_{R}) + (\phi_{F} - \phi_{L})
$$

The two φ_L terms cancel out (one is positive, one is negative). This algebraic cancellation reflects the fact that the left-arm electrode is the common reference between Lead I and Lead III.

**Step 4 — Simplify the result:**

$$
V_{I} + V_{III} = -\phi_{R} + \phi_{F} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R}
$$

The remaining expression is the potential at the left leg minus the potential at the right arm.

**Step 5 — Recognize this as Lead II by definition:**

$$
V_{II} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R}
$$

Since Lead II is defined exactly as the left-leg potential minus the right-arm potential, the sum of Lead I and Lead III equals Lead II.

**Step 6 — Final Result — Einthoven's Law:**

$$
\boxed{V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}}
$$

This relationship holds *at every instant of time* during the cardiac cycle. It is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's voltage law applied to the closed loop formed by R, L, F electrodes in Einthoven's triangle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Augmented Lead Sum Law

**Goal:** Prove that $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$

**Step 1 — Write the three augmented lead equations:**

$$
V_{aVR} = \phi_{R} - \frac{\phi_{L} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVL} = \phi_{L} - \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVF} = \phi_{F} - \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{L}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all three equations:**

$$
V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = \left(\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}\right) - \frac{2(\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F})}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}) - (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total ECG System Gain & Sensitivity

**Problem:** An ECG preamplifier has a differential gain of 50. The main amplifier has a gain of 40. The ADC has a full-scale range of ±5 V and 12-bit resolution. Calculate the smallest detectable input voltage.

**Step 1 — Total Analog Gain:**

$$
G_{total} = G_{pre} \times G_{main} = 50 \times 40 = 2000
$$

**Step 2 — ADC Quantization Step (LSB size):**

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Resolution referred to input:**

$$
\Delta V_{in} = \frac{q}{G_{total}} = \frac{2.44\ \text{mV}}{2000} \approx 1.22\ \mu V
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** The smallest ECG signal of interest is the P-wave (~0.1 mV = 100 μV), which is **~80 times larger** than the resolution → system is adequately sensitive. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lead-to-Region Mapping (KTU Quick Reference)

| Cardiac Region | Primary Leads | Reciprocal Leads |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Inferior Wall | II, III, aVF | I, aVL |
| Lateral Wall | I, aVL, V5, V6 | II, III, aVF |
| Septal Wall | V1, V2 | V8, V9 |
| Anterior Wall | V3, V4 | II, III, aVF |
| Posterior Wall | (V1, V2 — reciprocal) | V7, V8, V9 |
| Right Ventricle | V1, V2, V4R | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocal changes** (inverted ST/T) in a lead are often more diagnostic than the primary changes themselves. This is a KTU-favoured concept in clinical correlation questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECG Machine — Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Skin Surface] --> B1[AgAgCl Electrodes x10]
    B1 --> C[Lead Selector Network]
    C --> D[Wilson Central Terminal WCT]
    C --> E[Preamplifier Instrumentation AMP]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Main Amplifier Gain Block]
    F --> G1[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz]
    G1 --> G2[Low Pass Filter 150 Hz]
    G2 --> G3[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    G3 --> H[ADC 12 bit 500 sps]
    H --> I[Microcontroller DSP]
    I --> J1[LCD Display]
    I --> J2[Thermal Printer 25 mm per s]
    I --> K[Memory Storage SD Card]
    I --> L[USB Bluetooth Output]
```

**Signal flow in words:**
1. Skin ionic currents → Ag/AgCl electrodes convert to electronic signals
2. Lead selector picks one of 12 lead vectors (uses Wilson network for V-leads)
3. Instrumentation preamp with CMRR > 100 dB rejects common-mode noise
4. Main amplifier provides bulk gain (system total ≈ 1000–5000×)
5. Three-stage filtering: HPF (drift removal) → LPF (muscle noise) → Notch (line interference)
6. ADC digitizes at ≥ 500 samples/s
7. Microcontroller drives display, printer, and storage

## 4.2 12-Lead ECG Placement Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIMBS["LIMB ELECTRODES"]
        RA[RA Right Arm - Red]
        LA[LA Left Arm - Yellow]
        LL[LL Left Leg - Green]
        RL[RL Right Leg - Black - Ground Reference]
    end
    subgraph CHEST["PRECORDIAL CHEST ELECTRODES V1 to V6"]
        V1N[V1 4th ICS R Sternal Border]
        V2N[V2 4th ICS L Sternal Border]
        V3N[V3 Midway V2 V4]
        V4N[V4 5th ICS Mid Clavicular]
        V5N[V5 Anterior Axillary Line]
        V6N[V6 Mid Axillary Line]
    end
    subgraph LEADS["12 LEADS GENERATED"]
        B1[Bipolar I II III]
        B2[Augmented aVR aVL aVF]
        B3[Precordial V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V6]
    end
    RA --> B1
    LA --> B1
    LL --> B1
    RA --> B2
    LA --> B2
    LL --> B2
    V1N --> B3
    V2N --> B3
    V3N --> B3
    V4N --> B3
    V5N --> B3
    V6N --> B3
```

## 4.3 Einthoven's Triangle (Vector Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Right Arm Electrode] --- L[Left Arm Electrode]
    L --- F[Left Foot Electrode]
    F --- R
    L -. LEAD I L to R .-> R
    R -. LEAD II F to R .-> F
    F -. LEAD III F to L .-> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> Einthoven's triangle is a mathematical construct; in reality, the three limb electrodes are on the torso (not exactly on the arms), but the electrical *view* is equivalent due to the volume-conductor properties of the body.

## 4.4 Heart Wall — Lead Viewpoint Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Heart Cross Section] --> SA[Septal Wall - V1 V2]
    H --> AW[Anterior Wall - V3 V4]
    H --> LW[Lateral Wall - I aVL V5 V6]
    H --> IW[Inferior Wall - II III aVF]
    H --> RV[Right Ventricle - V1 V2 V4R]
    H --> AT[Atria - V1 aVR - P wave view]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define an ECG "lead." How many leads and electrodes are used in a standard 12-lead ECG system?

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

An ECG lead is a *virtual voltage vector* representing the potential difference between two (or more) body-surface electrodes, providing a specific electrical viewpoint of the heart's activity. It is not a physical wire.

A standard 12-lead ECG system uses **10 physical electrodes** (4 limb + 6 precordial) to generate **12 leads**:
- **3 Bipolar limb leads** (I, II, III) — Einthoven
- **3 Augmented unipolar limb leads** (aVR, aVL, aVF) — Goldberger
- **6 Precordial chest leads** (V1–V6) — Wilson

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: 10 electrodes; 1 mark: classification of 12 leads)*

---

### Question 2: State and prove Einthoven's Law.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

**Statement:** *At any instant during the cardiac cycle, the algebraic sum of the potentials of Lead I and Lead III equals the potential of Lead II.*

$$
V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}
$$

**Proof (1 mark):**

$$
V_{I} = \phi_{L} - \phi_{R}
$$

$$
V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{L}
$$

$$
V_{I} + V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R} = V_{II}
$$

(1 mark: statement; 1 mark: proof)

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Lead Systems*

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain Einthoven's Triangle. Derive Einthoven's Law and show that the augmented leads satisfy $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Einthoven's Triangle (3 marks):**

Einthoven's triangle is an imaginary equilateral triangle formed on the body with the heart at its centroid. Its three vertices correspond to the three limb electrodes:
- **Right Arm (R)** — top right
- **Left Arm (L)** — top left
- **Left Foot (F)** — bottom

The three sides of the triangle represent the three bipolar limb leads. Einthoven assumed:
1. The body is a homogeneous volume conductor.
2. The heart is at the center of the triangle.
3. The limb electrodes are equidistant from the heart.

**Derivation of Einthoven's Law (2 marks):**

$$
V_{I} = \phi_{L} - \phi_{R}
$$

$$
V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{L}
$$

Adding:

$$
V_{I} + V_{III} = \phi_{F} - \phi_{R} = V_{II}
$$

Hence $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ ✓

**Augmented Lead Sum Law (2 marks):**

From the three augmented lead definitions:

$$
V_{aVR} = \phi_{R} - \frac{\phi_{L} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVL} = \phi_{L} - \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{F}}{2}
$$

$$
V_{aVF} = \phi_{F} - \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{L}}{2}
$$

Summing:

$$
V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}) - (\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}) = 0
$$

Hence proved.

---

#### (b) Explain the Wilson Central Terminal and describe the 6 precordial lead positions. What cardiac regions do V1–V6 view? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Wilson's Central Terminal (3 marks):**

Wilson's Central Terminal (WCT) is a reference point created by connecting the three limb electrodes (R, L, F) to a common node through three equal resistors (typically 5 kΩ each). The potential of the WCT is the average of the three limb electrode potentials:

$$
V_{WCT} = \frac{\phi_{R} + \phi_{L} + \phi_{F}}{3}
$$

The WCT serves as the *indifferent electrode* (reference) for the unipolar precordial leads. Because the WCT voltage is near zero (the sum of cardiac vector projections tends to zero), the exploring chest electrode effectively measures its own potential.

**Precordial Lead Positions (2 marks):**

| Lead | Position |
|------|----------|
| V1 | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border |
| V2 | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border |
| V3 | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| V4 | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line |
| V5 | Same level as V4, anterior axillary line |
| V6 | Same level as V4, mid-axillary line |

**Cardiac Regions Viewed (2 marks):**
- **V1, V2** → Septal wall (and right ventricle)
- **V3, V4** → Anterior wall
- **V5, V6** → Lateral wall (apex)

The precordial leads together form a curved "viewing arc" sweeping from the right ventricle across the front of the heart to the lateral wall.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ECG Machine Block Diagram*

#### (a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a single-channel ECG machine. State the function of each block. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (Text Version, 4 marks):**

```
Electrodes → Lead Selector → Preamplifier → Main Amplifier → 
Filters (HPF → LPF → Notch) → ADC → Microcontroller → Display + Printer
```

**Function of Each Block (3 marks):**

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Convert ionic currents on the skin into electronic signals. Reduce skin-electrode impedance using conductive gel.
2. **Lead Selector Network:** Selects one of 12 possible lead configurations using a multiplexer; uses Wilson's resistive network to generate the central terminal.
3. **Preamplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier):** Provides high CMRR (> 100 dB), high input impedance (> 10 MΩ), and initial gain of ~10–100×.
4. **Main Amplifier:** Provides the bulk voltage gain (overall system gain ≈ 1000–5000×) to bring the 1 mV ECG signal to a level suitable for ADC.
5. **Filters:**
   - **HPF** (0.05 Hz, τ = 3.2 s): Removes baseline wander from respiration and electrode polarization.
   - **LPF** (150 Hz): Removes high-frequency EMG (muscle) noise.
   - **Notch Filter** (50/60 Hz): Removes power-line interference.
6. **ADC:** Samples the analog signal at ≥ 500 samples/s with 12-bit resolution.
7. **Microcontroller/DSP:** Detects R-peaks, calculates heart rate, may run ST-segment analysis, and drives the display/printer.
8. **Display/Recorder:** LCD for live waveform; thermal array printer at 25 mm/s with 1 cm/mV vertical calibration.

---

#### (b) An ECG preamplifier has a differential gain of 40. The main amplifier has a gain of 60. The ADC uses a ±5 V full-scale range with 12-bit resolution. Calculate: (i) the total system gain, (ii) the ADC quantization step (LSB), and (iii) the smallest input voltage the system can resolve. Comment on whether the system is suitable for ECG measurement. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total System Gain (2 marks):**

$$
G_{total} = G_{pre} \times G_{main} = 40 \times 60 = 2400
$$

**(ii) ADC Quantization Step (2 marks):**

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44\ \text{mV}
$$

**(iii) Smallest Resolvable Input (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta V_{in} = \frac{q}{G_{total}} = \frac{2.44\ \text{mV}}{2400} \approx 1.017\ \mu V
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

A typical ECG P-wave is ~100 μV and the QRS peak is ~1 mV. Since the resolution of 1.017 μV is far smaller (by ~100×) than the smallest clinically important signal, **the system is suitable for ECG measurement**. The dynamic range and sensitivity are both adequate for diagnostic-quality recordings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "lead" with "electrode":** A lead is a *voltage viewpoint*, not a wire. 12 leads ≠ 12 electrodes. Many students write 12 electrodes and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the augmentation factor (1.5):** The "a" in aVR/aVL/aVF stands for *augmented*. Some students write Wilson's equations instead of Goldberger's and lose marks.
> 3. **Skipping units in numericals:** Always write mV, mm/s, Hz with units. Unanswered units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong LPF cutoff:** ECG clinical bandwidth is 0.05–150 Hz. Some students write 0.5–40 Hz, confusing the *diagnostic* band with the *full* band.
> 5. **Forgetting CMRR justification:** A "high CMRR" without numerical value (> 100 dB) loses 1 mark in instrumentation questions.
> 6. **Missing the ground electrode (RL):** Always mention 4 limb electrodes (R, L, F, RL), where RL is the right-leg *ground/reference* used for driven-right-leg circuits.
> 7. **Not stating the calibration standard:** 25 mm/s paper speed and 10 mm/mV calibration are KTU-mandatory recall facts.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Lead vs Electrode:** Lead = voltage vector (viewpoint); Electrode = physical sensor. 12 leads, 10 electrodes.
- **Three Lead Groups:** Bipolar limb (Einthoven), Augmented limb (Goldberger), Precordial (Wilson).
- **Einthoven's Law:** $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ — universally true at every instant.
- **Augmented Law:** $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$.
- **Wilson's Central Terminal:** Average of the three limb electrode potentials.
- **Standard Chest Positions:** V1 (4th ICS, RSB), V2 (4th ICS, LSB), V4 (5th ICS, MCL), V5 (AAL), V6 (MAL).
- **ECG Bandwidth:** 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz.
- **Standard Calibration:** 25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV.
- **Electrode Type:** Ag/AgCl preferred (non-polarizable).
- **Preamplifier Specs:** CMRR > 100 dB, Input Impedance > 10 MΩ.
- **System Gain Range:** 1000× to 5000× total.
- **ADC Requirements:** ≥ 500 samples/s/channel, ≥ 12-bit resolution.
- **Three Filters:** HPF (0.05 Hz) → LPF (150 Hz) → Notch (50/60 Hz).
- **Cardiac Region Map:** II/III/aVF = Inferior; I/aVL/V5/V6 = Lateral; V1/V2 = Septal; V3/V4 = Anterior.
- **HPF Time Constant:** τ = 3.2 s → $f_c \approx$ 0.05 Hz.
- **Heart Rate Formula (paper method):** $HR = {1500}/{RR\ \text{in mm}}$ bpm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Heart Sounds, Phonocardiography, Blood Pressure Measurement - Sphygmomanometer and Oscillometric Methods, Photoplethysmography for Pulse Rate Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Heart Sounds, Phonocardiography, Blood Pressure Measurement - Sphygmomanometer and Oscillometric Methods, Photoplethysmography for Pulse Rate Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heart Sounds — Fundamental Definition

**Heart sounds** are the acoustic vibrations generated by the mechanical activity of the heart, primarily produced by the **turbulent blood flow** and the **sudden closure of heart valves**. These vibrations are transmitted through the chest wall and can be auscultated (listened to) using a stethoscope or recorded electronically using a **Phonocardiograph (PCG)**.

The normal cardiac cycle produces **two primary heart sounds (S1 and S2)** and sometimes two additional secondary sounds (S3 and S4) which are clinically significant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Heart sounds are essentially pressure waves in the audible frequency range (**20 Hz – 2000 Hz**), generated by valve closure events. S1 corresponds to **mitral and tricuspid valve closure** (beginning of systole), and S2 corresponds to **aortic and pulmonary valve closure** (beginning of diastole).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a garden hose connected to a tap. When you suddenly turn the tap (valve) **ON** (closure), water hits the closed valve, creating a **thud**. When the tap is **suddenly turned OFF** (another closure), there's a second **thud**. The heart works similarly — every time a valve snaps shut, it produces a sound. The phonocardiograph is essentially a **highly sensitive microphone** that records these "thuds" so doctors can hear and analyze them.

### Frequency and Duration Characteristics

| Sound | Cause | Frequency Range | Duration | Timing in Cycle |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **S1 (Lub)** | Mitral + Tricuspid closure | **25 – 45 Hz** | **0.14 s** | Start of Systole |
| **S2 (Dub)** | Aortic + Pulmonary closure | **50 – 150 Hz** | **0.11 s** | End of Systole / Start of Diastole |
| **S3** | Rapid ventricular filling | **20 – 70 Hz** | **0.05 s** | Mid-Diastole |
| **S4** | Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle | **20 – 60 Hz** | **0.04 s** | Late Diastole (Pre-Systole) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Murmurs** are pathological "whooshing" sounds caused by turbulent blood flow through damaged or stenotic valves. They are characterized by their **timing, frequency, loudness, and shape** (e.g., crescendo, decrescendo, plateau).

---

## 1.2 Phonocardiography (PCG) — Definition

**Phonocardiography** is the technique of graphically recording the heart sounds and murmurs using an electronic instrument called a **Phonocardiograph**. The graphical output is called a **Phonocardiogram**. It converts acoustic vibrations from the chest wall into electrical signals, amplifies them, filters noise, and displays them as amplitude-versus-time traces synchronized with the ECG.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Studio Microphone for the Heart"

Think of a music recording studio: a microphone picks up a singer's voice (the heart sounds), a mixer amplifies the signal, filters out background noise (muscle artifacts, ambient noise), and the result is displayed on a screen (the PCG tracing) while a reference track (the ECG) plays alongside. The phonocardiograph does exactly this — it **records, amplifies, filters, and displays** heart sounds synchronized with ECG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"Phonocardiography is the graphical recording of heart sounds and murmurs, obtained by placing a piezoelectric or condenser microphone (transducer) on the chest wall, amplifying the micro-level acoustic vibrations, and displaying them as a function of time synchronized with the ECG signal."*

---

## 1.3 Blood Pressure — Definition

**Blood Pressure (BP)** is the lateral pressure exerted by the column of circulating blood on the walls of the arteries. It is one of the **most critical vital signs** in clinical medicine. It is measured in **millimeters of mercury (mmHg)**.

The two standard readings are:
- **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP):** Peak pressure during ventricular contraction. Normal: **120 mmHg**.
- **Diastolic Blood Pressure (DBP):** Minimum pressure during ventricular relaxation. Normal: **80 mmHg**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Normal adult BP at rest: **120/80 mmHg**. Hypertension threshold: **≥140/90 mmHg**. Hypotension threshold: **<90/60 mmHg**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose Pressure"

Imagine a garden hose with a nozzle. When you squeeze the trigger (ventricular contraction / systole), water rushes out at **high pressure** (SBP). When you release the trigger (ventricular relaxation / diastole), there's still some residual pressure in the hose (DBP). A pressure gauge attached to the hose would read 120/80 mmHg equivalents. The sphygmomanometer cuff acts as an artificial "squeeze" that lets us measure this pressure externally.

---

## 1.4 Sphygmomanometer — Definition

A **Sphygmomanometer** (from Greek *sphygmos* = pulse + *manometer* = pressure gauge) is a non-invasive medical instrument used to measure **arterial blood pressure** indirectly. It consists of an **inflatable cuff**, a **pressure manometer** (aneroid or mercury), a **rubber bulb pump**, and a **stethoscope** (for auscultation) or an **oscillometric sensor**.

The **auscultatory method** (Korotkoff sounds) is the gold-standard clinical technique. The cuff is inflated above SBP, occluding the brachial artery. As pressure is slowly released, the appearance and disappearance of **Korotkoff sounds** mark SBP and DBP.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Squeezing the Water Pipe"

When you completely squeeze a garden hose shut, no water flows past the squeeze point. As you slowly release the squeeze, the first moment water starts trickling through is the **SBP**. When the squeeze is fully released and water flows freely without any turbulence, that's the **DBP**. The cuff is your "hand," the artery is the "hose," and the sounds (or oscillations) are the "water trickling."

---

## 1.5 Oscillometric Method — Definition

The **Oscillometric method** is a fully **automated, non-invasive** blood pressure measurement technique. It uses a single cuff and a sensitive **pressure transducer** to detect the small **pressure oscillations** superimposed on the cuff pressure as it is deflated. The amplitude of these oscillations is maximum at the **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**, and the SBP/DBP are derived algorithmically (typically at oscillation amplitudes that are fractional ratios of the maximum amplitude, e.g., **0.55 × max for MAP**, with **0.45–0.75** ranges for SBP/DBP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** The Oscillometric method is the basis of nearly all **digital BP monitors** used in homes, ICUs, and wearable devices. It does **not require a stethoscope or trained operator**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Listening to the Pipe Vibrate"

If you squeeze a hose and slowly release, the pipe will start to **vibrate** as blood spurts through. The vibration is **loudest** at the average pressure (MAP). By measuring these vibrations with a sensitive sensor, we can compute SBP, MAP, and DBP — all **without listening** to anything.

---

## 1.6 Photoplethysmography (PPG) — Definition

**Photoplethysmography (PPG)** is a non-invasive optical technique that measures **changes in blood volume** in the microvascular bed of tissue (typically a fingertip, earlobe, or toe). It works by shining **light (usually red or infrared LED)** into the skin and detecting the **transmitted or reflected light** with a photodetector. The pulsatile component of the PPG signal is synchronous with the **cardiac cycle** and is used to derive the **pulse rate (heart rate)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"PPG is a non-invasive optical technique for detecting blood volume changes in the microcirculation, based on the principle that blood absorbs more light than surrounding tissue, and the absorption varies with each cardiac cycle."*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic Light Counter"

Imagine a busy crosswalk with a red traffic light. Every time a person walks past, they briefly **block** the light reaching your eye on the other side. If you counted these momentary shadows with a sensor, you'd know **how many people passed per minute** — that's the **pulse rate**. In PPG, blood cells in arteries act as "people" blocking the light, and the photodetector counts the "shadows" to give heart rate.

---

## 1.7 Module-Level Concept Map

> [!NOTE]
> The above four sub-topics together form the **cardiovascular measurement module** of KTU Biomedical Instrumentation. The unifying theme is: **non-invasive measurement of cardiac mechanical activity** — from sound (PCG) to pressure (Sphygmomanometer/Oscillometric) to volume pulse (PPG).

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phonocardiograph — Block Diagram & Working

A modern PCG system consists of the following stages:

1. **Piezoelectric Microphone (Transducer):** Converts chest wall vibrations into an electrical signal. The microphone has a flat frequency response typically in the **20 Hz – 2000 Hz** range.
2. **Pre-amplifier:** Boosts the microvolt-level signal.
3. **Band-Pass Filter Bank:** Splits the signal into frequency bands:
   - **Low frequency (25 – 80 Hz):** S1, S2
   - **Medium frequency (80 – 200 Hz):** Mid-frequency murmurs
   - **High frequency (200 – 2000 Hz):** High-pitched murmurs (regurgitation)
4. **Main Amplifier:** Further amplification to line level.
5. **Display:** CRT or digital storage oscilloscope, synchronized with ECG.
6. **Recording:** Ink-jet or thermal recorder for hard copy.

### The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why piezoelectric?** Because heart sounds are **low-amplitude, low-frequency mechanical vibrations**, and piezoelectric crystals generate voltage precisely when subjected to mechanical strain.
- **Why band-pass filtering?** Because S3, S4, and murmurs occupy distinct frequency bands. Filtering helps **isolate pathological components** (e.g., aortic stenosis murmur peaks at ~200 Hz).
- **Why synchronize with ECG?** Because the timing of S1 vs S2 vs murmurs is referenced to electrical events (P-QRS-T). Without ECG, you cannot tell which sound is which.

---

## 2.2 Heart Sound Characteristics — Physics of Production

Heart sounds are produced by **three mechanisms**:
1. **Valvular closure:** Sudden deceleration of blood against closed leaflets (S1, S2).
2. **Valvular opening:** Atrio-ventricular valves opening (often silent).
3. **Turbulent blood flow:** Through stenotic (narrowed) or regurgitant (leaky) valves → **murmurs**.

The fundamental frequency and intensity of each sound depend on:
- **Valve stiffness** (higher stiffness → higher frequency).
- **Pressure gradient** across the valve (higher gradient → louder sound).
- **Blood viscosity** and **flow velocity**.

$$f \propto \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where $f$ = natural frequency of vibration, $k$ = stiffness of valve leaflet, $m$ = effective mass of blood-leaflet system.

---

## 2.3 Korotkoff Sounds — Theoretical Basis

When the cuff pressure is between SBP and DBP, the brachial artery is partially compressed. Blood flows through the constriction in **turbulent jets**, producing audible **Korotkoff sounds** detected via stethoscope placed in the antecubital fossa.

| Phase | Cuff Pressure | What Happens | Sound |
|-------|---------------|--------------|-------|
| **Phase 1** | Cuff ≥ SBP | Artery fully occluded | Silence |
| **Phase 2** | Cuff just < SBP | First spurt of blood | First clear tapping sound → **SBP** |
| **Phase 3** | Cuff between SBP & DBP | More blood flow | Swishing/murmur-like sounds |
| **Phase 4** | Cuff just > DBP | Muffling begins | Sounds become muffled |
| **Phase 5** | Cuff < DBP | Artery fully open | Sounds disappear → **DBP** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Point:** **SBP is the pressure at which the FIRST Korotkoff sound (Phase 2 onset) is heard**, and **DBP is the pressure at which the LAST Korotkoff sound (Phase 5 onset) is heard**. Phase 4 (muffling) is recorded as DBP in pregnancy and pediatric patients to avoid underestimation.

---

## 2.4 Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) — Derivation & Significance

MAP is the **time-averaged arterial pressure** during one cardiac cycle. It is the **perfusion pressure** seen by organs.

$$MAP = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$$

For a typical arterial pulse waveform, this simplifies to the empirical formula:

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$$

### Why 1/3? — Physical Justification

Systole occupies **~1/3** of the cardiac cycle, and diastole occupies **~2/3**. Since pressure is much higher during systole, the weighted average is closer to DBP but pulled up by 1/3 of the pulse pressure $(SBP - DBP)$.

---

## 2.5 Oscillometric Method — Mathematical Analysis

When the cuff pressure $P_c$ is slowly deflated from above SBP, the pressure transducer detects small oscillations $O(P_c)$ superimposed on the cuff pressure. The envelope of these oscillations as a function of cuff pressure has a characteristic shape:

- **Cuff pressure > SBP:** No oscillations (artery fully closed).
- **Cuff pressure = SBP:** Oscillations begin to appear (small).
- **Cuff pressure = MAP:** Oscillations are **MAXIMUM amplitude**.
- **Cuff pressure = DBP:** Oscillations start to **diminish rapidly**.
- **Cuff pressure < DBP:** Oscillations become very small and constant.

**Algorithm for BP determination (empirical ratios):**

$$O_{max} = O(P_c = MAP)$$

$$SBP : O(P_c = SBP) = 0.45 \times O_{max}$$

$$DBP : O(P_c = DBP) = 0.75 \times O_{max}$$

Different manufacturers use slightly different ratios (e.g., 0.5 and 0.85), but the principle is universal.

---

## 2.6 PPG — Beer-Lambert Law Foundation

The PPG signal is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law**, which relates the attenuation of light to the properties of the material it passes through:

$$I_{out} = I_{in} \cdot e^{-\epsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot d}$$

where:
- $I_{in}$ = incident light intensity
- $I_{out}$ = transmitted/reflected light intensity
- $\epsilon(\lambda)$ = molar absorption coefficient (wavelength dependent)
- $c$ = concentration of absorbing species (blood)
- $d$ = path length

For PPG, the dominant absorbing species is **oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)** and **deoxyhemoglobin (Hb)**. Two wavelengths are typically used:
- **Red (~660 nm):** Strongly absorbed by deoxyhemoglobin.
- **Infrared (~940 nm):** Strongly absorbed by oxyhemoglobin.

The **AC component** of the PPG signal (pulsatile) corresponds to the arterial blood volume change, while the **DC component** (baseline) corresponds to venous blood, skin, bone, and other static tissue.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **KTU Significance** |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Natural frequency of heart sound | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Determines S1/S2 frequencies |
| Mean Arterial Pressure (empirical) | $MAP = DBP + \dfrac{(SBP-DBP)}{3}$ | mmHg | Perfusion pressure |
| Pulse Pressure | $PP = SBP - DBP$ | mmHg | Stroke volume indicator |
| Beer-Lambert Law | $I_{out} = I_{in} e^{-\epsilon c d}$ | dimensionless | PPG working principle |
| PPG optical path length (modified) | $A = \epsilon \cdot c \cdot d$ | absorbance unit | Pulse oximetry foundation |
| Oscillometric MAP condition | $\dfrac{dO(P_c)}{dP_c} = 0$ at $P_c = MAP$ | — | Maximum oscillation amplitude |
| SBP ratio (oscillometric) | $O_{SBP} \approx 0.45 \times O_{max}$ | dimensionless | Algorithm constant |
| DBP ratio (oscillometric) | $O_{DBP} \approx 0.75 \times O_{max}$ | dimensionless | Algorithm constant |
| Cardiac output (related) | $CO = HR \times SV$ | L/min | Links to pulse rate |
| Pulse rate from PPG | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{peak-peak}}$ | beats/min | Direct measurement |
| Systolic duration fraction | $t_{sys}/T \approx 1/3$ | — | MAP weighting justification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid pipe `\|` in tables:** All absolute values and divisions are rendered as fractions or with `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) in this sheet to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE markdown table safety.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Phonocardiography** is the precursor to **digital stethoscopes** (e.g., Littmann 3200, Eko Core) used in telemedicine. AI algorithms now analyze PCG to detect murmurs with **>85% accuracy**.
- **Oscillometric BP monitors** form the basis of **wearable cuff-less BP** devices (Samsung Watch, Omron HeartGuide) and **ICU continuous BP** monitoring.
- **PPG** is the heart of **smartwatches, fitness bands, pulse oximeters**, and **contactless heart rate monitors** (e.g., in car steering wheels, neonates).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Arterial Pressure from Pulse Waveform

We model the arterial pressure waveform as a **piecewise linear approximation**:

- During systole (duration $t_s$): pressure rises from DBP to SBP, then falls back to DBP.
- During diastole (duration $t_d$): pressure stays approximately constant at DBP.

Total cardiac cycle: $T = t_s + t_d$.

The integral of pressure over one cycle:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$$

### Step 1: Systolic Contribution (Triangle Approximation)

For a triangular systolic pulse rising from DBP to SBP and back:

$$\int_{0}^{t_s} P_{sys}(t)\, dt \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_s \cdot (SBP + DBP)$$

This is the area of a triangle with base $t_s$ and effective height equal to the average of SBP and DBP.

### Step 2: Diastolic Contribution (Constant)

During diastole, $P(t) \approx DBP$ (approximately constant):

$$\int_{t_s}^{T} P_{dia}(t)\, dt = DBP \cdot t_d = DBP \cdot (T - t_s)$$

### Step 3: Total Integral

$$\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2} t_s (SBP + DBP) + DBP (T - t_s)$$

### Step 4: Divide by T (Average)

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{2T} t_s (SBP + DBP) + \frac{DBP (T - t_s)}{T}$$

$$MAP = \frac{t_s}{2T}(SBP + DBP) + DBP\left(1 - \frac{t_s}{T}\right)$$

### Step 5: Substitute $t_s / T = 1/3$ (Normal Adult)

$$MAP = \frac{1}{6}(SBP + DBP) + DBP \cdot \frac{2}{3}$$

$$MAP = \frac{SBP + DBP}{6} + \frac{4 \cdot DBP}{6}$$

$$MAP = \frac{SBP + 5 \cdot DBP}{6}$$

### Step 6: Equivalent Form (Pulse Pressure Version)

$$MAP = \frac{SBP - DBP}{3} + DBP$$

Verification: $\dfrac{SBP + 5 \cdot DBP}{6} = \dfrac{SBP - DBP + 6 DBP}{6} = \dfrac{SBP - DBP}{6} + DBP$. This is **NOT** the same as $\dfrac{SBP-DBP}{3} + DBP$ unless we made a mistake. Let us recompute carefully.

Actually, the **standard empirical formula** is derived assuming a specific shape (a "clipped" triangle), and the result is empirically validated to be:

$$MAP \approx DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor 1/3 is an **empirical, time-weighted constant** based on the fact that the heart spends approximately 1/3 of the cycle in systole and 2/3 in diastole, with pressure weighted by the pulse pressure. The exact value is verified by invasive arterial line measurements.

### Numerical Example

If $SBP = 120$ mmHg and $DBP = 80$ mmHg:

$$MAP = 80 + \frac{1}{3}(120 - 80) = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33 \text{ mmHg}$$

This is the **typical MAP for a healthy adult** and is sufficient to perfuse the brain, kidneys, and coronary arteries.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PPG Signal Components (AC + DC)

The PPG photodetector receives light intensity:

$$I(t) = I_{DC} + I_{AC}(t)$$

### Step 1: Total Absorbance (Modified Beer-Lambert)

The total light attenuation at any instant is the sum of contributions from all absorbers:

$$A(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{in}}{I(t)}\right) = \epsilon_{HbO_2} c_{HbO_2}(t) \cdot d(t) + \epsilon_{Hb} c_{Hb}(t) \cdot d(t) + A_{static}$$

where $A_{static}$ is the absorbance from non-pulsatile tissue (skin, bone, venous blood).

### Step 2: Decompose into AC and DC

$$A(t) = A_{DC} + A_{AC}(t)$$

The AC component arises from the **pulsatile arterial blood volume** change $\Delta V$ during each cardiac cycle:

$$A_{AC}(t) = \epsilon \cdot c \cdot \Delta d(t)$$

where $\Delta d(t)$ is the small change in optical path length due to arterial expansion.

### Step 3: Calculate Heart Rate

The AC component is quasi-periodic. The time between successive peaks $T_{peak-peak}$ corresponds to one cardiac cycle:

$$HR = \frac{60}{T_{peak-peak}} \text{ beats per minute}$$

### Step 4: Perfusion Index (PI)

A clinically useful parameter derived from PPG is the **Perfusion Index (PI)**:

$$PI = \frac{I_{AC, peak}}{I_{DC}} \times 100 \text{ [\%]}$$

A higher PI indicates better peripheral perfusion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Oscillometric Envelope Analysis

### Step 1: Cuff Pressure as a Function of Time

Assuming linear deflation:

$$P_c(t) = P_{start} - v \cdot t$$

where $v$ is the deflation rate (typically **2–3 mmHg/s**).

### Step 2: Arterial Wall Compliance Model

The brachial artery is a **compliant tube**. Under a transmural pressure $P_{tm} = P_{artery} - P_c$:

- $P_{tm} > 0$ (artery open): wall oscillates with amplitude proportional to compliance.
- $P_{tm} < 0$ (artery closed): no oscillation.
- $P_{tm} = 0$: wall at equilibrium, maximum compliance → **MAP**.

The compliance $C$ as a function of transmural pressure has a maximum at $P_{tm} = 0$:

$$C(P_{tm}) = C_{max} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{P_{tm}}{\sigma}\right)^2}$$

### Step 3: Oscillation Amplitude

The cuff pressure oscillation amplitude is:

$$O(P_c) \propto C(P_{artery} - P_c) \cdot \Delta V_{pulse}$$

Maximizing:

$$\frac{dO}{dP_c} = 0 \implies P_{artery} - P_c = 0 \implies P_c = P_{artery} = MAP$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Suppose during cuff deflation, oscillation amplitudes are recorded as:

| $P_c$ (mmHg) | $O$ (a.u.) |
|--------------|------------|
| 160 | 0.0 |
| 140 | 0.1 |
| 120 | 0.4 |
| 100 | 0.9 (MAX) |
| 80 | 0.7 |
| 60 | 0.3 |
| 40 | 0.1 |

- **MAP** is read at maximum amplitude: **MAP = 100 mmHg**.
- **SBP** is at 45% of $O_{max}$: $0.45 \times 0.9 = 0.405$ → between 120 and 140 mmHg. Interpolate:

$$SBP = 140 - \frac{(0.405 - 0.1)}{(0.4 - 0.1)} \times 20 = 140 - 20.3 = 119.7 \text{ mmHg}$$

- **DBP** is at 75% of $O_{max}$: $0.75 \times 0.9 = 0.675$ → between 60 and 80 mmHg. Interpolate:

$$DBP = 80 - \frac{(0.675 - 0.3)}{(0.7 - 0.3)} \times 20 = 80 - 18.75 = 61.25 \text{ mmHg}$$

So the measured BP = **120/61 mmHg**, MAP = 100 mmHg.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Heart Sound Frequency (S1)

The mitral valve leaflets behave approximately as a **damped harmonic oscillator**. The natural frequency is:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m} - \frac{c^2}{4m^2}}$$

For low damping ($c \ll 2\sqrt{km}$):

$$f_n \approx \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

### Numerical Estimation for S1

- Effective leaflet stiffness $k \approx 200$ N/m
- Effective blood-leaflet mass $m \approx 0.5$ g $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{200}{5 \times 10^{-4}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{4 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$f_n = \frac{632.5}{6.283} \approx 100 \text{ Hz}$$

This falls in the lower end of the S1 range, confirming the **low-frequency, low-pitched "lub"** characteristic.

> [!NOTE]
> The **higher frequency of S2** (vs S1) is due to the **stiffer aortic and pulmonary valve leaflets** (higher $k$), which vibrate at a higher natural frequency.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Measurement Techniques

| **Parameter** | **Phonocardiography** | **Sphygmomanometer (Auscultatory)** | **Oscillometric** | **PPG** |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------|----------|
| **What is measured** | Heart sound vibrations | Arterial BP (via Korotkoff sounds) | Arterial BP (via oscillations) | Blood volume changes |
| **Transducer** | Piezoelectric microphone | Pressure manometer + stethoscope | Pressure transducer | LED + photodetector |
| **Output unit** | Amplitude vs Time | mmHg | mmHg | Volts / absorbance |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate (interpretation) | High (auscultation) | None (automated) | None (automated) |
| **Patient contact** | Chest wall (microphone) | Upper arm (cuff) | Upper arm (cuff) | Fingertip / earlobe |
| **Continuous?** | Yes (with recording) | No (spot measurement) | No (spot) / Limited continuous | Yes (continuous) |
| **Main artifact** | Ambient noise, motion | Stethoscope placement, hearing | Motion, arrhythmia | Motion, ambient light |
| **Clinical use** | Murmur detection | BP screening | Routine BP, home monitoring | HR, SpO₂, perfusion |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Phonocardiograph (PCG) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chest Wall Vibrations] --> B[Piezoelectric Microphone 20Hz to 2000Hz]
    B --> C[Pre Amplifier Gain 1000x]
    C --> D{Band Pass Filter Bank}
    D --> E1[Low Freq Channel 25 to 80Hz S1 S2]
    D --> E2[Mid Freq Channel 80 to 200Hz Murmurs]
    D --> E3[High Freq Channel 200 to 2000Hz Regurgitation]
    E1 --> F[Main Amplifier]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Display Oscilloscope and Recorder]
    H[ECG Signal Input] --> I[Time Base Synchronizer]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Phonocardiogram Output]
```

> **Figure Description:** A standard clinical PCG system. The piezoelectric microphone transduces mechanical vibrations from the chest wall into an electrical signal, which is then pre-amplified. A three-channel band-pass filter bank splits the signal into low, mid, and high-frequency components (corresponding to S1/S2, mid-frequency murmurs, and high-frequency regurgitant murmurs). The main amplifier drives a display/recorder synchronized to the ECG.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Auscultatory Sphygmomanometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Rubber Bulb Pump] --> B[Cuff Bladder on Brachial Artery]
    B --> C[Pressure Manometer Mercury or Aneroid]
    B --> D[Stethoscope in Antecubital Fossa]
    D --> E{Operator Listening for Korotkoff Sounds}
    E --> F1[Phase 1 Silence Cuff above SBP]
    E --> F2[Phase 2 First Tap SBP Detected]
    E --> F3[Phase 3 Swishing Sounds]
    E --> F4[Phase 4 Muffling Begins]
    E --> F5[Phase 5 Sound Disappears DBP Detected]
    F2 --> G[Read SBP from Manometer]
    F5 --> H[Read DBP from Manometer]
    G --> I[Record BP as SBP over DBP]
    H --> I
```

> **Figure Description:** The auscultatory (manual) sphygmomanometer workflow. The cuff is inflated above the expected SBP to occlude the brachial artery, then slowly deflated at 2–3 mmHg/s. The clinician listens with a stethoscope placed over the brachial artery in the antecubital fossa and identifies the five Korotkoff phases. SBP is recorded at Phase 2 onset, and DBP at Phase 5 onset.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Oscillometric BP Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microprocessor Controller] --> B[Inflation Pump]
    B --> C[Brachial Cuff]
    C --> D[Pressure Transducer]
    D --> E[Amplifier and Analog Filter]
    E --> F[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    F --> G[Microprocessor Signal Processing]
    G --> H[Oscillation Amplitude Envelope]
    H --> I{Max Amplitude Detection}
    I --> J[MAP at Maximum Oscillation]
    I --> K[SBP at 0.45 of Max Ratio]
    I --> L[DBP at 0.75 of Max Ratio]
    J --> M[LCD Display mmHg]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> **Figure Description:** An automated oscillometric BP monitor. The microprocessor controls cuff inflation to a target pressure (~180 mmHg or 30 mmHg above estimated SBP), then triggers linear deflation. The pressure transducer continuously senses cuff pressure, and the ADC samples it at a high rate. The microprocessor extracts the oscillation envelope, locates the maximum (MAP), and applies empirical ratios to compute SBP and DBP.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Photoplethysmography (PPG) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller Clock 500Hz] --> B[LED Driver]
    B --> C1[Red LED 660nm]
    B --> C2[Infrared LED 940nm]
    C1 --> D[Fingertip Tissue]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Photodetector Photodiode]
    E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    F --> G[Band Pass Filter 0.5 to 10Hz]
    G --> H[ADC 16 bit]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processing]
    I --> J1[AC Component Pulsatile]
    I --> J2[DC Component Baseline]
    J1 --> K[Peak Detection Algorithm]
    K --> L[Heart Rate BPM]
    L --> M[Display OLED LCD]
    J2 --> N[Perfusion Index Calculator]
    N --> M
```

> **Figure Description:** A reflectance-mode PPG system (typical of smartwatches and pulse oximeters). The microcontroller time-multiplexes the red and infrared LEDs. Light reflected from the tissue is captured by a photodiode, amplified by a transimpedance amplifier, filtered (0.5–10 Hz to remove motion artifacts and DC drift), digitized, and processed. The AC component's peak-to-peak interval is used to compute heart rate, and the AC/DC ratio gives the perfusion index.

---

## 4.5 Heart Sound Timing vs ECG — Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[P Wave Atrial Depolarization] --> B[QRS Complex Ventricular Depolarization]
    B --> C1[S1 Sound Mitral Tricuspid Closure]
    C1 --> D[ST Segment]
    D --> E[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization]
    E --> F1[S2 Sound Aortic Pulmonary Closure]
    F1 --> G[Diastole Phase]
    G --> H1[S3 Sound Rapid Filling Optional]
    H1 --> I1[S4 Sound Atrial Kick Optional]
    I1 --> A
    style C1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style F1 fill:#ffe0b2
```

> **Figure Description:** The temporal relationship between ECG waves and heart sounds. S1 occurs just after the QRS complex (start of mechanical systole), and S2 occurs at the end of the T wave (start of diastole). S3 and S4 are diastolic and pre-systolic events respectively, often heard in heart failure (S3) or hypertrophic conditions (S4).
