## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define Bremsstrahlung radiation. Explain how it differs from characteristic X-ray radiation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bremsstrahlung Radiation (Braking Radiation):** It is a type of X-ray produced when high-speed electrons are **decelerated and deflected** by the strong Coulomb field of target nuclei. The electron loses kinetic energy, which is emitted as an X-ray photon. It produces a **continuous spectrum** with photon energies ranging from 0 to a maximum equal to the entire kinetic energy of the incident electron.

**Characteristic X-ray Radiation:** Produced when an incident electron **ejects an inner-shell electron** (e.g., K-shell) of the target atom. An outer-shell electron drops to fill the vacancy, emitting a photon with **discrete, characteristic energy** (e.g., Kα, Kβ lines).

| Feature | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| Spectrum | Continuous | Discrete lines |
| Origin | Coulomb deceleration | Shell transitions |
| Depends on | $V$ (voltage) | $Z$ (atomic number) |

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark differences + 1 mark table/comparison)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] State and explain the Duane-Hunt Law. What is its significance in X-ray production?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Duane-Hunt Law:** The minimum wavelength $\lambda_{min}$ of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the accelerating voltage $V$ applied across the tube.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{hc}{eV} = \frac{1.24}{V(\text{in kV})} \text{ nm}$$

**Significance:**
1. It sets the **theoretical lower limit** of X-ray wavelength that can be produced at a given tube voltage.
2. It confirms the **quantum nature** of X-ray production (photons have discrete energies).
3. It is used to **calculate the maximum photon energy**: $E_{max} = eV$ (e.g., at 100 kVp, $E_{max} = 100$ keV).
4. It provides experimental verification of the **Planck-Einstein relation** $E = h\nu$.

(3 marks: 1 mark statement + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a diagnostic X-ray machine. List the major subsystems and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of X-ray Machine** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete diagram).

A diagnostic X-ray machine consists of the following major subsystems:

1. **X-ray Tube (Coolidge Type):** The heart of the system. A vacuum-sealed diode with a tungsten filament cathode and a tungsten target anode. The filament is heated to ~2500 K, emitting electrons via thermionic emission. These electrons are accelerated by a high voltage (30–150 kV) toward the angled anode. Upon striking the target, the electrons produce X-rays via bremsstrahlung and characteristic mechanisms.

2. **High-Voltage Generator:** A step-up transformer increases the 220 V AC mains supply to 30–150 kV. A full-wave rectifier converts this AC to high-voltage DC (constant potential). The high voltage determines the X-ray quality (penetrating power).

3. **Filament Transformer:** A step-down transformer provides low voltage (4–6 V) and high current (3–5 A) to heat the filament, controlling the tube current (mA) and thus the X-ray quantity.

4. **Control Console:** The operator interface for setting kVp, mA, and exposure time. Modern consoles are microprocessor-based and provide automatic exposure control (AEC).

5. **Rectifier Unit:** Silicon high-voltage diodes arranged in a full-wave bridge configuration to provide constant DC potential to the tube, reducing voltage ripple and improving X-ray beam quality.

6. **Collimator Assembly:** Adjustable lead shutters that restrict the X-ray beam to the area of clinical interest, reducing patient dose and scatter radiation.

7. **Filtration System:** Typically 2.5 mm aluminum equivalent, which removes low-energy X-ray photons that would only contribute to patient skin dose without reaching the image receptor.

8. **Cooling System:** Essential because 99% of input energy is converted to heat. Methods include oil bath, water cooling, or rotating anodes.

9. **Image Receptor:** Either a film-screen cassette or a digital flat-panel detector (FPD) that records the X-ray intensity pattern.

10. **Image Processing & Display:** Digital systems include post-processing, storage in PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System), and display on medical-grade monitors.

**Working Principle:** When the operator initiates exposure, the filament is heated, the high voltage is applied, electrons accelerate from cathode to anode, strike the target, and produce X-rays. The X-ray beam is filtered, collimated, passes through the patient, and forms an image on the receptor.

(7 marks: 2 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation of subsystems + 2 marks working principle)

---

**Q. (b)** An X-ray tube operates at a peak voltage of 100 kV with a tube current of 80 mA for 0.05 seconds. Calculate (i) the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted, (ii) the HVL in soft tissue ($\mu = 0.18$ cm⁻¹), and (iii) the X-ray production efficiency for a tungsten target. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Tube voltage $V = 100$ kV $= 100{,}000$ V
- Tube current $I = 80$ mA $= 0.080$ A
- Exposure time $t = 0.05$ s
- $\mu = 0.18$ cm⁻¹
- $Z_{tungsten} = 74$

**(i) Minimum wavelength (Duane-Hunt Law):**

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.24}{V(\text{kV})} \text{ nm}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.24}{100}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = 0.0124 \text{ nm} = 1.24 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 0.124 \text{ Å}}$$

**(ii) Half-Value Layer (HVL):**

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.18}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HVL} = 3.85 \text{ cm}}$$

**Verification:** After 3.85 cm of soft tissue, intensity is halved. After 7.7 cm, it is $1/4$, and so on.

**(iii) X-ray production efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1.1 \times 10^{-9} \times Z \times V$$

$$\eta = 1.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 74 \times 100{,}000$$

$$\eta = 8.14 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 0.814\% \approx 0.81\%}$$

**Conclusion:** Only about 0.81% of the input electrical energy is converted to X-rays; the remaining 99.19% is wasted as heat in the anode. This justifies the need for high-capacity cooling systems in X-ray tubes.

(7 marks: 2 marks part (i) + 2 marks part (ii) + 3 marks part (iii))

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the principles of X-ray generation. Describe the construction and working of a Coolidge X-ray tube with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principles of X-ray Generation:**

X-rays are produced when high-velocity electrons, accelerated through a high potential difference, strike a metal target. The energy conversion occurs via two atomic-level mechanisms:

**1. Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation):**
- High-speed electrons pass near target nuclei and are **decelerated** by the strong Coulomb attraction.
- The **lost kinetic energy** is emitted as an X-ray photon.
- Produces a **continuous X-ray spectrum** with photon energies from 0 to $E_{max} = eV$.
- Governed by the **Duane-Hunt Law**: $\lambda_{min} = hc/(eV) = 1.24/V$ nm.

**2. Characteristic X-ray Radiation:**
- An incident electron with sufficient energy **ejects an inner-shell electron** (e.g., K-shell) of a target atom.
- An electron from a higher shell (L or M) drops to fill the vacancy.
- The **energy difference** is emitted as a photon of discrete, characteristic energy.
- For tungsten target: Kα = 59.3 keV, Kβ = 67.2 keV.
- Governed by **Moseley's Law**: $\nu \propto (Z - \sigma)^2$.

**Construction of Coolidge X-ray Tube:**

The Coolidge tube (invented 1913) is a **hot-cathode, high-vacuum diode** consisting of:

1. **Cathode:** A coiled tungsten filament (0.2 mm diameter) mounted inside a molybdenum focusing cup. Heated to ~2500 K by a low-voltage filament current (4–6 V, 3–5 A), producing thermionic electrons.

2. **Anode:** A tungsten target (small disc) embedded in a massive copper block. The target face is beveled at 7–20° (line focus principle). A rotating anode design uses a tungsten-rhenium disc rotating at 3,000–10,000 rpm to distribute heat over a larger area.

3. **Glass/Metal-Ceramic Envelope:** Provides vacuum ($10^{-6}$ torr) and electrical insulation. Modern tubes use metal-ceramic for higher heat ratings.

4. **High-Voltage Cable Connections:** Insulated cables carry 30–150 kV from the high-voltage generator to the tube terminals.

**Working:**
- The filament is heated → thermionic emission produces an electron cloud.
- A high voltage (kVp) is applied between cathode and anode.
- Electrons are accelerated across the vacuum toward the anode.
- Upon striking the tungsten target, the electrons produce X-rays via both bremsstrahlung and characteristic mechanisms.
- X-rays exit through a thin beryllium window (low attenuation).
- The useful X-ray beam is collimated and filtered before reaching the patient.

**Advantages of Coolidge Tube:**
- Independent control of X-ray quality (kVp) and quantity (mA).
- Stable and reproducible output.
- High intensity and long tube life.

(7 marks: 2 marks principles + 2 marks construction with diagram + 2 marks working + 1 mark advantages)

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail the various interactions of X-rays with matter. Explain the Beer-Lambert Law and derive an expression for the Half-Value Layer (HVL). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Interactions of X-rays with Matter:**

When X-ray photons traverse biological tissue, they interact with atoms via three primary mechanisms:

**1. Photoelectric Effect (Dominant at Low Energies < 50 keV):**
- The X-ray photon is **completely absorbed** by a tightly bound inner-shell electron (typically K-shell).
- The photon transfers all its energy to the electron, which is ejected as a **photoelectron**.
- The atom is left in an excited state; subsequent shell transitions produce **secondary characteristic X-rays** or **Auger electrons**.
- **Attenuation coefficient dependence:** $\mu_{PE} \propto Z^3/E^3$ (strongly dependent on atomic number).
- **Clinical significance:** Primary source of radiographic contrast because bone ($Z \approx 13.8$) absorbs ~5× more than soft tissue ($Z \approx 7.4$) at 50 keV.

**2. Compton Scattering (Dominant at 50–100 keV):**
- The X-ray photon interacts with a **loosely bound outer-shell (free) electron**.
- The photon scatters with reduced energy and altered direction; the electron recoils.
- This is an **inelastic collision**; the scattered photon has longer wavelength (lower energy).
- **Attenuation coefficient:** $\mu_C \propto Z/E$ (linear in $Z$).
- **Clinical significance:** Major source of **image-degrading scatter radiation**. Controlled using **grids** (Bucky) between patient and detector.

**3. Pair Production (NOT relevant for diagnostic X-rays):**
- For photon energy > 1.022 MeV (twice the rest mass energy of an electron).
- The photon converts to an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus.
- Not used in diagnostic radiography but relevant in **radiotherapy** (LINACs) and high-energy physics.

**Beer-Lambert Law (Linear Attenuation Law):**

When a parallel, monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a material of thickness $x$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

**Derivation of HVL:** (refer to Section 3.2 for detailed derivation)

By definition, HVL is the thickness $x_{1/2}$ at which $I = I_0/2$:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 e^{-\mu \cdot x_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu \cdot x_{1/2}}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln(1/2) = -\mu \cdot x_{1/2}$$

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HVL} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}}$$

**Conclusion:** The Beer-Lambert law governs image formation in radiography. The HVL is a clinically useful parameter for characterizing beam quality and tissue penetration. For diagnostic X-rays, the HVL of soft tissue is typically 3–4 cm, and of bone is ~1–2 cm.

(7 marks: 3 marks interactions + 2 marks Beer-Lambert law + 2 marks HVL derivation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake in $\lambda_{min}$ formula:** Students often confuse units. Always convert voltage to **kV** before using $\lambda_{min} = 1.24/V$ in nm. For $V$ in volts, use $\lambda_{min} = 1.24 \times 10^{-6}/V$ in meters.
> 2. **Mixing up Bremsstrahlung with Characteristic:** Bremsstrahlung is **continuous**; characteristic is **discrete lines**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer:** Always state units (nm, Å, cm, mA·s, etc.) — a numerical answer without units loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **HVL vs Tenth-Value Layer (TVL):** TVL = $\ln 10/\mu = 2.303 \times$ HVL. Don't confuse them.
> 5. **In Block Diagrams:** Every box must have a label and arrows showing direction of signal/power flow. Unlabeled blocks lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Efficiency Formula:** The constant $k = 1.1 \times 10^{-9}$ is in SI units; if asked for percentage, multiply by 100.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X-ray Radiography — Key Facts to Memorize for KTU Exams:**

✅ **Discovery:** X-rays discovered by **Wilhelm Röntgen** on **8 Nov 1895**; first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901).

✅ **Two Production Mechanisms:**
- **Bremsstrahlung** = continuous spectrum, from electron deceleration
- **Characteristic** = discrete lines, from inner-shell transitions

✅ **Duane-Hunt Law:** $\lambda_{min}$ (nm) $= 1.24 / V$ (kV) — *must memorize this formula!*

✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = 1.1 \times 10^{-9} \cdot Z \cdot V$ — *typically < 1% for diagnostic tubes*

✅ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — *governs X-ray attenuation*

✅ **HVL Formula:** HVL $= 0.693 / \mu$ — *independent of incident intensity*

✅ **Three Interactions:** Photoelectric ($Z^3/E^3$), Compton ($Z/E$), Pair production (not diagnostic)

✅ **Quality vs Quantity:** **kVp** controls quality (penetrating power), **mA × s** controls quantity (number of photons)

✅ **X-ray Tube (Coolidge):** Vacuum tube, tungsten filament (~2500 K), tungsten target, 30–150 kV across tube

✅ **Block Diagram Subsystems:** HV transformer, filament transformer, rectifier, control console, collimator, filter (2.5 mm Al), cooling, image receptor

✅ **15% Rule:** Increasing kVp by 15% doubles the X-ray exposure

✅ **Anode Angle & Line Focus:** Effective focal spot $= f \cdot \sin\theta$ (typically $\theta = 7°$–$20°$)

✅ **Moseley's Law:** Characteristic X-ray frequency $\nu \propto (Z - \sigma)^2$

✅ **Photoelectric effect** is the **primary contrast mechanism** in radiography (strong $Z$-dependence)

✅ **Compton scatter** is the **primary cause of image degradation** — reduced by using **antiscatter grids**

✅ **mAs (milliampere-seconds)** is the **direct measure of X-ray quantity**

✅ **Wavelength of diagnostic X-rays:** 0.1 to 0.5 Å (energies 25–120 keV)

✅ **Why tungsten target?** Highest practical Z (74), highest melting point (3422°C), good thermal conductivity

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Tomography (CT) - Principle of Operation, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) - Basic Principle, Ultrasound Imaging, Basic Principles - Ultrasonic Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computed Tomography (CT) - Principle of Operation, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) - Basic Principle, Ultrasound Imaging, Basic Principles - Ultrasonic Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Computed Tomography (CT)

**Formal Definition:** Computed Tomography (CT) is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that employs a rotating fan or cone beam of X-ray photons, acquiring multiple angular projection profiles (sinograms) of the patient, which are then computationally reconstructed using mathematical algorithms (Radon Transform, Filtered Back-Projection) to generate high-resolution, cross-sectional (tomographic) grayscale images of internal anatomical structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CT imaging relies on differential X-ray attenuation by tissues of varying physical density and atomic number. The reconstructed image is mapped in standardized Hounsfield Units (HU).

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Bread Loaf" Analogy):** Imagine a loaf of bread. If you can only photograph the whole loaf, you cannot see the raisins inside. But if you slice the loaf into thin uniform slices and photograph each slice individually, you can locate every raisin precisely. CT does exactly this to the human body — it virtually "slices" the patient into thin cross-sectional tomographic images (typical slice thickness: 0.5 mm to 10 mm) by mathematically reconstructing X-ray attenuation data from hundreds of angular projections.

---

### 1.2 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

**Formal Definition:** Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a radiation-free diagnostic imaging technique based on the principle of Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), where hydrogen nuclei (protons) within the patient's body are aligned by a strong static magnetic field (B₀), perturbed by a Radio-Frequency (RF) pulse, and the resulting relaxation signal (Free Induction Decay) is spatially encoded using gradient magnetic fields to construct detailed tomographic images of soft tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for MRI:** 
> - **Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen (γ): 42.58 MHz/T** 
> - Standard clinical B₀: **1.5 T or 3.0 T**
> - Larmor frequency for hydrogen at 1.5 T: **63.87 MHz**

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Spinning Top in a Magnet" Analogy):** Think of hydrogen protons in your body as billions of tiny spinning tops. When you place them in a strong magnetic field (like the Earth's gravity for a top), they align with the field. Now, if you "flick" them with a radio wave pulse tuned to a specific frequency (Larmor frequency), they tip over and wobble. As they wobble back to alignment, they emit faint radio signals. MRI detects and maps these signals to construct images of where the hydrogen is concentrated (which is everywhere, but especially in water and fat).

---

### 1.3 Ultrasound Imaging

**Formal Definition:** Ultrasound Imaging is a real-time, non-ionizing diagnostic imaging modality that transmits high-frequency mechanical sound waves (1 MHz – 20 MHz) into the body using a piezoelectric transducer and processes the reflected echoes (A-mode, B-mode, M-mode) to generate visual representations of internal anatomical structures based on differences in acoustic impedance at tissue interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Ultrasound Frequency Range:** 
> - **2–5 MHz**: Deep abdominal and obstetric imaging 
> - **5–10 MHz**: Vascular and small parts imaging 
> - **10–20 MHz**: Superficial (skin, eye, intravascular) imaging

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Submarine SONAR" Analogy):** A submarine in the ocean sends out a "ping" sound wave. By measuring how long it takes for the echo to return from objects (fish, the seabed, another sub), and from which direction, the submarine maps its surroundings. Ultrasound imaging works identically — the transducer pings the body with high-frequency sound, listens for echoes bouncing off tissue boundaries (organs, blood, bone), and constructs a 2D map of those boundaries.

---

### 1.4 Ultrasonic Transducers

**Formal Definition:** An ultrasonic transducer is an electroacoustic energy conversion device that exploits the **piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880) to interconvert electrical energy and mechanical acoustic energy at ultrasonic frequencies, functioning as both transmitter (transmitting mode) and receiver (receiving mode) in a pulse-echo ultrasound system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Most Common Piezoelectric Material:** **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** due to its high electromechanical coupling coefficient, high Curie temperature, and tunable dielectric properties.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Drumhead" Analogy):** A piezoelectric crystal acts like a microscopic drumhead. When you apply an alternating voltage to it, it vibrates (drumstick hits the drum) and emits a sound wave. Conversely, when a returning sound wave hits it, the crystal vibrates and produces a small voltage (the drumhead creates a tiny electrical signal). One crystal, two jobs — ping and listen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 CT — Principle of Operation Step-by-Step

1. **X-ray Generation:** A high-voltage X-ray tube (typically 80–140 kVp) emits a collimated, fan-shaped or cone-shaped beam of X-ray photons.
2. **Attenuation through Tissue:** As the beam passes through the patient, photons are absorbed and scattered differentially based on the linear attenuation coefficient **μ(x, y)** of each tissue voxel.
3. **Detection:** An arc-shaped array of solid-state detectors (e.g., scintillator + photodiode) on the opposite side measures the attenuated intensity **I**.
4. **Rotation & Projection:** The gantry (X-ray tube + detectors) rotates 360° around the patient, acquiring hundreds of projection profiles at discrete angles θ.
5. **Data Storage:** Each profile is stored as a row in a sinogram (Radon space).
6. **Image Reconstruction:** A computer applies the **Radon Transform** followed by **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** or iterative reconstruction to compute μ(x, y) at every voxel.
7. **Display:** Voxel values are normalized to **Hounsfield Units (HU)** and displayed as a 2D grayscale image on the monitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** CT is the gold standard for trauma imaging (head, chest, abdomen), pulmonary embolism, oncology staging, and CT angiography. Modern multi-slice CT (64–320 slice) can image the entire heart in a single heartbeat.

---

### 2.2 MRI — Theoretical Steps

1. **Magnetization Alignment:** Patient placed in bore of superconducting magnet (B₀ = 1.5 T). Hydrogen protons precess about B₀ at the **Larmor frequency**, their net magnetization vector (M₀) aligned with B₀.
2. **RF Excitation:** A 90° RF pulse at the Larmor frequency tips M₀ into the transverse (x-y) plane.
3. **Free Precession & Dephasing:** Protons precess at Larmor frequency, but rapidly dephase (lose phase coherence) due to spin-spin interactions, causing transverse magnetization M_xy to decay with time constant **T2**.
4. **Longitudinal Recovery:** Simultaneously, protons realign with B₀, and longitudinal magnetization M_z recovers with time constant **T1**.
5. **Signal Detection:** The decaying transverse magnetization induces a voltage in the receive coil — the **Free Induction Decay (FID)** signal.
6. **Spatial Encoding:** Three gradient coils (X, Y, Z) impose linear magnetic field variations, encoding spatial position into signal frequency (readout gradient), phase (phase-encode gradient), and slice selection (slice-select gradient).
7. **Image Reconstruction:** The signal is digitized, stored in **k-space** (Fourier domain), and inverse Fourier transformed to produce the image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MRI excels at soft tissue:** Different tissues have characteristic T1 and T2 values, allowing exquisite differentiation between gray matter, white matter, CSF, fat, muscle, and pathological lesions.

---

### 2.3 Ultrasound Imaging — Theoretical Steps

1. **Pulse Generation:** A high-voltage pulser (typically ±100 V, nanosecond duration) excites the piezoelectric crystal, producing a short (~2 cycle) acoustic pulse.
2. **Transmission:** The pulse travels through the body at the speed of sound in tissue (~1540 m/s).
3. **Reflection (Echo Generation):** At each tissue interface (where acoustic impedance Z changes), a portion of the wave reflects back.
4. **Reception:** The same transducer (in pulse-echo mode) detects returning echoes and converts them to voltage signals.
5. **Time-of-Flight Calculation:** Depth of reflector: **d = c·t/2**, where c is the speed of sound in tissue and t is the round-trip time.
6. **Signal Processing:** Echoes are amplified (Time Gain Compensation — TGC), envelope-detected, and converted to brightness levels (B-mode = Brightness mode).
7. **Display:** Brightness-modulated dots are mapped to (x, y) coordinates derived from the transducer element firing sequence, forming a 2D real-time image at frame rates of 15–60 Hz.

---

### 2.4 Ultrasonic Transducers — Design & Operation

A clinical ultrasound transducer is a multi-layered assembly:

- **Piezoelectric Element:** PZT disc, typically thickness = λ/2 to resonate at the desired fundamental frequency.
- **Matching Layer:** Quarter-wavelength (λ/4) layer of acoustic impedance intermediate between PZT (Z ≈ 30 MRayl) and tissue (Z ≈ 1.5 MRayl) to maximize transmission efficiency.
- **Backing/Damping Block:** Heavily attenuating epoxy-tungsten composite that absorbs backward-radiated acoustic energy, shortening the pulse and improving axial resolution.
- **Housing & Electrical Contacts:** Shielded enclosure with coaxial cable connection.

The transducer operates in **pulse-echo mode** — the pulser applies a voltage pulse, then the system switches to receive mode for the brief listening window before the next pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Higher frequency → better resolution but shallower penetration. Lower frequency → deeper penetration but coarser resolution. The optimal frequency balances the clinical requirement.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Standard Units** |
|-------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Beer-Lambert Attenuation | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Attenuated X-ray intensity | $I$ in photons/s, $x$ in cm, $\mu$ in cm⁻¹ |
| Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) | $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Normalized tissue attenuation | Dimensionless (HU) |
| Larmor Frequency | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Precession frequency of proton | $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T, $B_0$ in Tesla |
| Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | Resistance to acoustic wave propagation | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $c$ in m/s, $Z$ in Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| Intensity Reflection Coefficient | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Fraction of intensity reflected at interface | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Intensity Transmission Coefficient | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Fraction of intensity transmitted | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Depth of Reflector | $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | Round-trip ultrasound ranging | $d$ in m, $c$ in m/s, $t$ in s |
| Acoustic Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength in tissue | $\lambda$ in m, $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue |
| Transducer Resonant Frequency (Thickness Mode) | $f_0 = \dfrac{c_p}{2 \, d}$ | PZT disc fundamental frequency | $f_0$ in Hz, $d$ in m, $c_p$ = PZT sound velocity |
| Axial Resolution | $R_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, \Delta f}$ | Minimum separable axial distance | $R_{axial}$ in m, $\Delta f$ in Hz (bandwidth) |
| SNR (MRI) | $SNR \propto B_0^{\,7/4}$ (approx.) | Signal-to-noise scaling with field | Power-law dependence |
| Free Induction Decay (FID) | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t / T_2^*}$ | Transverse magnetization decay | $t$ in s, $T_2^*$ in s |
| T1 Recovery | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t / T_1}\right)$ | Longitudinal regrowth | $t$ in s, $T_1$ in s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examiner's Note:** In KTU board exams, when asked to compute the Hounsfield Unit, ALWAYS state explicitly: *"By definition, water has HU = 0 and air has HU = −1000."* This single sentence often earns the full 3 marks for definition. For ultrasound, ALWAYS mention c = 1540 m/s in soft tissue — the examiner expects this constant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) from Beer-Lambert Law

**Starting Point:** The Beer-Lambert law for X-ray attenuation through a uniform medium is:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity
- $I$ = transmitted X-ray intensity after traversing thickness $x$
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient of the medium (cm⁻¹)

**Step 1: Define the Hounsfield Scale Calibration Anchors**

By international convention, the CT scale is calibrated to two reference materials:
- **Water:** $\mu_{water}$ is assigned HU = 0
- **Air:** $\mu_{air} \approx 0$ is assigned HU = −1000

**Step 2: Establish a Linear Normalization**

Since HU must be a linear function of $\mu$ (so that voxel values are physically additive and reproducible across scanners), we write:

$$HU = a \cdot \mu + b$$

**Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions**

Apply the calibration for water:

$$0 = a \cdot \mu_{water} + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -a \cdot \mu_{water}$$

Apply the calibration for air ($\mu_{air} \approx 0$):

$$-1000 = a \cdot \mu_{air} + b \approx 0 + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -1000$$

**Step 4: Solve for Constants a and b**

From the air calibration: $b = -1000$

Substitute into the water calibration:

$$0 = a \cdot \mu_{water} - 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \dfrac{1000}{\mu_{water}}$$

**Step 5: Construct the Final Hounsfield Equation**

$$HU = \dfrac{1000}{\mu_{water}} \cdot \mu - 1000 = 1000 \left( \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}}$$

**Verification:** 
- For water: $HU = 1000 \times (1 - 1) = 0$ ✓
- For air (where $\mu_{air} \approx 0$): $HU = 1000 \times (0 - 1) = -1000$ ✓
- For dense bone ($\mu \approx 2 \mu_{water}$): $HU = 1000 \times (2 - 1) = +1000$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because CT numbers are normalized to water, they are **scanner-independent** — a liver lesion measuring HU = 65 will have the same number whether measured in Kerala, Tokyo, or New York. This is critical for longitudinal patient follow-up and multi-center clinical trials.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Acoustic Impedance Matching Layer Thickness

**Problem:** A PZT transducer (Z_PZT = 30 MRayl) must transmit ultrasound efficiently into soft tissue (Z_tissue = 1.5 MRayl). Determine the optimal thickness and acoustic impedance of a quarter-wave matching layer.

**Step 1: Condition for Maximum Transmission (Impedance Matching)**

For a single quarter-wave matching layer, maximum power transmission occurs when the matching layer's acoustic impedance equals the geometric mean of the source and load impedances:

$$Z_{match} = \sqrt{Z_{PZT} \cdot Z_{tissue}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Z_match**

$$Z_{match} = \sqrt{30 \times 1.5} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.71 \text{ MRayl}$$

**Step 3: Quarter-Wave Thickness Condition**

The matching layer must be exactly a quarter-wavelength thick at the operating frequency to ensure constructive interference of transmitted waves:

$$t_{match} = \dfrac{\lambda_{match}}{4} = \dfrac{c_{match}}{4 f_0}$$

**Step 4: Example Numerical Evaluation**

For a 5 MHz transducer and assuming the matching layer material has $c_{match} = 3000$ m/s:

$$t_{match} = \dfrac{3000}{4 \times 5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{3000}{20 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** Modern transducers often use **two matching layers** (dual matching) to achieve even broader bandwidth and smoother impedance transition. This is the engineering reason for the multi-layer construction of clinical probes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Larmor Frequency for Clinical MRI

**Starting Point:** The Larmor equation relates the precession frequency of a magnetic moment in a magnetic field to the field strength:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the angular precession frequency.

**Step 1: Solve for f_0 in Hertz**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Gyromagnetic Ratio of Hydrogen**

For ¹H (proton): $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad/(T·s)}$

**Step 3: Evaluate for 1.5 T Clinical Scanner**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{2.675 \times 10^8 \times 1.5}{2 \pi} = \dfrac{4.0125 \times 10^8}{6.2832} \approx 63.87 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 63.87 \text{ MHz at 1.5 T}}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate for 3.0 T Clinical Scanner**

$$f_0 = 2 \times 63.87 = 127.74 \text{ MHz}$$

**Verification Table:**

| **Field Strength B₀ (T)** | **Larmor Frequency f₀ (MHz)** | **Clinical Application** |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 0.5 | 21.29 | Open low-field scanners |
| 1.0 | 42.58 | Extremity scanners |
| **1.5** | **63.87** | **Standard clinical imaging** |
| 3.0 | 127.74 | High-resolution neuroimaging, fMRI |
| 7.0 | 298.06 | Research only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The RF coil must be tuned precisely to the Larmor frequency. A 1 ppm mistuning (e.g., due to patient loading or temperature drift) can cause significant signal loss. Modern MRI systems use **active frequency tuning** and **B₀ shimming** to maintain resonance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Intensity Reflection at a Tissue Interface

**Problem:** An ultrasound wave traveling in liver (Z₁ = 1.66 × 10⁶ Rayl) strikes a gallstone (Z₂ = 7.8 × 10⁶ Rayl). Calculate the fraction of intensity reflected.

**Step 1: Apply Reflection Coefficient Formula**

$$R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$R = \left( \dfrac{7.8 - 1.66}{7.8 + 1.66} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{6.14}{9.46} \right)^2$$

**Step 3: Compute Numerator and Denominator**

$$R = (0.6490)^2 = 0.4212$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 42.1\%}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

42% of the incident intensity is reflected at this interface — which is why **gallstones appear as bright hyperechoic structures with acoustic shadowing** in clinical ultrasound. The remaining 57.9% is transmitted onward.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the SQUARE in the reflection coefficient formula. Pressure reflection coefficient is $(Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$, but INTENSITY reflection coefficient (which has units of power/area) is the SQUARE of the pressure coefficient. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

---

### 3.5 Numerical: Ultrasound Depth Calculation

**Problem:** A 3.5 MHz pulse-echo ultrasound system detects an echo returning 130 μs after transmission. Find the depth of the reflector in soft tissue. (c = 1540 m/s)

**Step 1: Apply the Round-Trip Ranging Equation**

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$d = \dfrac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \dfrac{0.2002}{2} = 0.1001 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 10 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The factor of 2 in the denominator is the **single most forgotten element** in depth calculations. Without it, your answer will be exactly DOUBLE the true depth.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — CT Scanner System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HVGenerator80to140kVp] --> Tube[XrayTubeFanBeam]
    Tube --> Col[Collimator]
    Col --> Pat[PatientTableGantry]
    Pat --> Det[DetectorArcArray]
    Det --> DAQ[DataAcquisitionSystem]
    DAQ --> ADC[AnalogtoDigitalConverter]
    ADC --> Recon[ReconstructionComputerFilteredBackProjection]
    Recon --> Display[CTConsoleDisplay512x512Matrix]
    GantryCtrl[GantryRotationController] --> Tube
    GantryCtrl --> Det
    Tube -.->|Rotation| Gantry360[Full360DegreeRotation]
    Operator[Radiologist] --> Display
    Display --> PACS[PACSServerArchive]
```

> **KTU Interpretation Tip:** When asked to draw a CT block diagram, you MUST show: HV generator → X-ray tube → patient → detectors → ADC → reconstruction computer → display. Mention gantry rotation as a separate control loop. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — MRI System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Magnet[SuperconductingMagnetB0equals15T] --> Patient[PatientInBore]
    Gradient[GradientCoilsXYZ] --> Patient
    RFTransmit[RFTransmitCoil] -->|90degreePulseatLarmorFrequency| Patient
    Patient -->|FIDSignal| RFReceive[RFReceiveCoilArray]
    RFReceive --> Preamp[LowNoisePreamplifier]
    Preamp --> ADC2[AnalogtoDigitalConverter]
    ADC2 --> KSpace[KSpaceFourierDomainStorage]
    KSpace --> FFT[Inverse2D3DFourierTransform]
    FFT --> Recon2[ImageReconstruction]
    Recon2 --> Console[MRIConsoleDisplay]
    Sequencer[PulseSequenceController] --> Gradient
    Sequencer --> RFTransmit
    Shim[ShimCoilsB0Homogenization] --> Magnet
    Console --> DICOM[DICOMPACSNetwork]
```

> **KTU Interpretation Tip:** The "heart" of MRI is the **Pulse Sequencer**, which orchestrates gradient and RF events with microsecond precision. In a 14-mark question, always mention: magnet system, gradient coils, RF coils, computer, and pulse sequencer.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Pulse-Echo Ultrasound Imaging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[TXBeamformerPulser] --> TRX[TransducerPiezoelectricPZT]
    TRX -->|AcousticPulse| Body[PatientTissue]
    Body -->|Echoes| TRX
    TRX --> RX[RXPreamplifier]
    RX --> TGC[TimeGainCompensation]
    TGC --> BeamformerRX[RXBeamformer]
    BeamformerRX --> Detector[EnvelopeDetector]
    Detector --> ScanConv[ScanConverterPolarToCartesian]
    ScanConv --> DisplayUS[RealTimeDisplayBmode]
    Master[MasterClockSyncController] --> TX
    Master --> RX
    User[Sonographer] --> DisplayUS
    DisplayUS --> DICOMUS[DICOMStorage]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Transducer Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Housing[ExternalPlasticHousing] --> Shield[ElectricalRFIshielding]
    Shield --> Backing[BackingBlockHeavyEpoxyTungsten]
    Backing --> Electrode2[RearElectrode]
    Electrode2 --> PZT[LeadZirconateTitanatePZTDisc]
    PZT --> Electrode1[FrontElectrode]
    Electrode1 --> Match[QuarterWaveMatchingLayer]
    Match --> Lens[AcousticLensFocalization]
    Match --> PatientContact[PatientContactFace]
    Coax[CoaxialCableConnector] --> Electrode2
    Coax --> Electrode1
    Electrode1 -.->|Transmitvoltagedrive| PZT
    PZT -.->|Receiveroutputvoltage| Coax
```

---

### 4.5 Functional Flow — CT Image Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PatientScanning] --> B[RawProjectionProfiles1Darrays]
    B --> C[Sinogram2Ddatasettheta_vs_p]
    C --> D[LogarithmicTransformation]
    D --> E[RampFilter1DConvolution]
    E --> F[BackProjectionSmearing2D]
    F --> G[VoxelGridSuperposition]
    G --> H[HounsfieldScaling]
    H --> I[Final512x512CTImage]
    I --> J[WindowLevelAdjustment]
    J --> K[DiagnosticDisplay]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The **Ramp Filter** (step E) is essential — without it, back-projection produces a blurred image due to the 1/r singularity in the Radon transform inverse. The ramp filter is the mathematical core of the famous **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Hounsfield Unit. State the typical HU values for water, air, fat, muscle, and dense bone.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a quantitative scale used in CT to express radiodensity, normalized to the attenuation of water:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

Typical values:

| **Tissue** | **HU Value** |
|------------|--------------|
| Air | −1000 |
| Fat | −100 to −50 |
| Water | 0 (by definition) |
| CSF | +15 |
| Muscle | +35 to +55 |
| White matter | +20 to +30 |
| Gray matter | +37 to +45 |
| Blood (clotted) | +60 to +80 |
| Bone (cortical) | +700 to +3000 |

(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for water/air anchors, 1 for typical tissue values)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Larmor frequency? Calculate its value for a 1.5 T MRI system for hydrogen protons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Larmor frequency** ($f_0$) is the precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment about an external static magnetic field $B_0$:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

For hydrogen (¹H), the gyromagnetic ratio is $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T.

At $B_0 = 1.5$ T:

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for numerical evaluation)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

## ✦ QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**Q3(a).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the principle of operation of a Computed Tomography (CT) scanner with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The CT scanner operates on the principle of differential X-ray attenuation combined with computer-assisted tomographic reconstruction.

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 of these notes)

**Working Principle:**

1. **X-ray Generation:** A high-voltage generator (80–140 kVp) drives an X-ray tube that emits a collimated fan-shaped or cone-shaped X-ray beam.
2. **Attenuation:** The beam passes through the patient. Different tissues attenuate X-rays based on their linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$ and thickness, following the Beer-Lambert law: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
3. **Detection:** An arc-shaped array of solid-state detectors (typically 700–1000 elements in a 3rd-generation geometry) measures the transmitted intensity $I$ from hundreds of angles.
4. **Gantry Rotation:** The X-ray tube and detector array rotate 360° around the patient (1 rotation in ~0.3 s in modern scanners), acquiring a complete sinogram of projection data.
5. **Data Acquisition:** Each projection is a 1D profile of attenuation values. The set of all projections is the **sinogram** in Radon space $(p, \theta)$.
6. **Reconstruction:** The computer applies the **inverse Radon transform** — practically implemented as **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** — to compute $\mu(x,y)$ at every voxel.
7. **Hounsfield Scaling:** Voxel values are converted to HU using the standard formula and displayed as a 2D grayscale image on a 512×512 monitor.

**Key Advantages over conventional radiography:**
- Eliminates superimposition of overlying structures
- Provides true cross-sectional anatomy
- Provides quantitative HU values for tissue characterization

(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for stepwise operation, 1 for HU conversion, 1 for advantages)

---

**Q3(b).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Derive the expression for the Hounsfield Unit (CT number) and explain its significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (See complete derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes)

Starting from Beer-Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$

By definition, HU is linearly proportional to $\mu$:

$$HU = a\mu + b$$

Applying calibration anchors:
- Water ($\mu = \mu_{water}$): $HU = 0 \Rightarrow b = -a\mu_{water}$
- Air ($\mu \approx 0$): $HU = -1000 \Rightarrow b = -1000$

Solving simultaneously:

$$\boxed{HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}}$$

**Significance:**

1. **Universal Calibration:** HU is scanner-independent — water is always 0 HU and air is always −1000 HU, regardless of scanner manufacturer or kVp setting.
2. **Tissue Differentiation:** Different tissues have characteristic HU ranges (e.g., acute hemorrhage: 50–80 HU; chronic hemorrhage: 0–30 HU), enabling diagnosis.
3. **Contrast Enhancement:** Iodinated contrast media (μ ≈ 3× water) raise blood HU from ~40 to ~200–400, allowing visualization of vasculature.
4. **Window/Level Adjustment:** Radiologists can window the HU range to optimize display for specific tissues (lung window: −1000 to −500 HU; bone window: +400 to +1500 HU).
5. **Quantitative Treatment Planning:** Radiation therapy planning uses HU-electron density conversion curves for accurate dose calculation.

(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for tissue HU values, 2 for clinical significance)

---

## ✦ QUESTION B (Alternative Choice for the same 14 marks)

**Q4(a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the basic principle of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) with a block diagram. List the main components of an MRI system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** MRI is based on **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen protons (abundant in body water and fat). When placed in a strong static magnetic field $B_0$, the proton magnetic moments precess at the **Larmor frequency** $f_0 = \gamma B_0$. A 90° RF pulse at $f_0$ tips the net magnetization into the transverse plane; as protons relax back to equilibrium, they emit a **Free Induction Decay (FID)** signal that is detected and spatially encoded.

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.2 of these notes)

**Main Components:**

1. **Main Magnet (Superconducting):** Produces homogeneous $B_0$ (typically 1.5 T or 3.0 T). Operates at 4 K using liquid helium cryogen.
2. **Gradient Coils (X, Y, Z):** Three orthogonal coils that impose linear field variations for slice selection, phase encoding, and frequency encoding.
3. **RF Transmit Coil:** Generates the 90° (and 180°) excitation pulses at Larmor frequency.
4. **RF Receive Coil:** Detects the FID signal. Modern phased-array coils improve SNR.
5. **Shim Coils:** Correct $B_0$ inhomogeneities for image fidelity.
6. **Pulse Sequencer:** Computer-controlled timing of RF and gradient events.
7. **Computer & Reconstruction System:** Performs inverse Fourier transform from k-space to image space.
8. **Patient Table & Bore:** With safety interlock system.

**Image Formation Steps:**
- Slice selection (slice-select gradient + frequency-selective RF pulse)
- Phase encoding (phase-encode gradient pulse)
- Frequency encoding (readout gradient during signal acquisition)
- Repetition for N phase-encode steps to fill k-space

(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for block diagram, 3 for components and steps)

---

**Q4(b).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain T1 and T2 relaxation in MRI. How do they contribute to image contrast? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**T1 Relaxation (Longitudinal or Spin-Lattice Relaxation):**

After a 90° RF pulse tips the magnetization into the transverse plane, the longitudinal component $M_z$ gradually recovers toward its equilibrium value $M_0$ by transferring energy to the surrounding molecular lattice ("spin-lattice" energy exchange).

The recovery follows:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t / T_1}\right)$$

**T1** is defined as the time required for $M_z$ to recover to 63% of $M_0$.

- **Short T1 tissues** (e.g., fat, ~250 ms at 1.5 T) — recover quickly, appear **bright on T1-weighted images**.
- **Long T1 tissues** (e.g., CSF, ~4000 ms at 1.5 T) — recover slowly, appear **dark on T1-weighted images**.

**T2 Relaxation (Transverse or Spin-Spin Relaxation):**

After excitation, the transverse magnetization $M_{xy}$ decays because individual spins dephase due to magnetic interactions between neighboring spins ("spin-spin" interactions).

The decay follows:

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t / T_2}$$

**T2** is defined as the time required for $M_{xy}$ to decay to 37% of $M_0$.

- **Long T2 tissues** (e.g., CSF, ~2000 ms) — decay slowly, appear **bright on T2-weighted images**.
- **Short T2 tissues** (e.g., muscle, ~50 ms) — decay rapidly, appear **dark on T2-weighted images**.

**Image Contrast Generation:**

| **Sequence Type** | **TR** | **TE** | **Weighting** | **Clinical Use** |
|-------------------|--------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| T1-weighted | Short (~500 ms) | Short (~15 ms) | T1 contrast | Anatomy, post-contrast |
| T2-weighted | Long (~4000 ms) | Long (~90 ms) | T2 contrast | Pathology, edema, tumors |
| Proton density | Long | Short | Proton density | Intermediate contrast |

where TR = repetition time and TE = echo time.

**Key Insight:** Pathology (e.g., tumors, inflammation, edema) typically has **prolonged T1 and T2** because of increased free water content — this is why T2-weighted imaging is the workhorse of clinical MRI for disease detection.

(7 marks: 2 for T1 explanation, 2 for T2 explanation, 2 for table of contrast, 1 for clinical significance)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 4 Examinations:**
> 
> **1. CT Questions:**
> - Forgetting to mention the **gantry rotation** mechanism (lose 1 mark).
> - Not stating the Beer-Lambert law equation explicitly (lose 1 mark).
> - Confusing "slice thickness" with "voxel size" — they are related but not identical.
> - Forgetting the **calibration anchors** (water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU) when deriving HU (lose 1 mark).
> 
> **2. MRI Questions:**
> - Confusing **T1 and T2** — the most common error. Remember: T1 is about **energy exchange with lattice (longitudinal)**; T2 is about **dephasing among spins (transverse)**.
> - Forgetting to mention the **Larmor frequency** in any MRI question.
> - Not stating $\gamma$ value for hydrogen (42.58 MHz/T).
> - Omitting the role of **gradient coils** in spatial encoding — many students describe only the magnet and RF system.
> 
> **3. Ultrasound Questions:**
> - Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the round-trip depth equation $d = ct/2$.
> - Confusing **pressure reflection coefficient** (no square) with **intensity reflection coefficient** (squared).
> - Not mentioning **c = 1540 m/s** in soft tissue — this is a mandatory constant.
> - Confusing **axial resolution** with **lateral resolution** (axial depends on pulse length; lateral depends on beam width).
> 
> **4. Transducer Questions:**
> - Failing to mention the **piezoelectric effect** as the operating principle.
> - Not explaining the role of the **backing block** (damping) or **matching layer** (impedance matching).
> - Confusing **transmitter mode** vs **receiver mode** — the same crystal does both, separated in time.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

#### CT (Computed Tomography)
- ✅ CT = **Computed Tomography** = digital cross-sectional X-ray imaging
- ✅ Core physics: **Beer-Lambert law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$
- ✅ Scale: **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**, with water = 0, air = −1000
- ✅ Reconstructed via **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** from sinogram
- ✅ Components: HV generator, X-ray tube, gantry, detectors, ADC, reconstruction computer, display
- ✅ Modern: **multi-slice helical/spiral CT**, 64–320 detector rows
- ✅ No moving parts for patient (only gantry rotates)
- ✅ **IONIZING radiation** — uses X-rays

#### MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)
- ✅ MRI = **Magnetic Resonance Imaging** based on **NMR of hydrogen (¹H)**
- ✅ Key constant: **γ_¹H = 42.58 MHz/T** (gyromagnetic ratio)
- ✅ Larmor frequency: $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ (e.g., 63.87 MHz at 1.5 T)
- ✅ Two key relaxation times: **T1 (longitudinal, spin-lattice)** and **T2 (transverse, spin-spin)**
- ✅ Spatial encoding via **gradient coils** in X, Y, Z directions
- ✅ Image reconstructed via **inverse Fourier transform** of **k-space** data
- ✅ **NO ionizing radiation** — uses RF + magnetic field
- ✅ Contraindication: **ferromagnetic implants**, pacemakers
- ✅ Excellent for **soft tissue** differentiation (brain, muscle, tumors)

#### Ultrasound Imaging
- ✅ **Non-ionizing**, real-time, portable, inexpensive
- ✅ Frequency range: **2–20 MHz** diagnostic
- ✅ Operating mode: **pulse-echo** (transmit, then receive)
- ✅ Depth: $d = \frac{ct}{2}$ where $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue
- ✅ Modes: **A-mode (amplitude)**, **B-mode (brightness)**, **M-mode (motion)**
- ✅ Image formed from **acoustic impedance (Z = ρc) differences**
- ✅ Reflection coefficient: $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$

#### Ultrasonic Transducers
- ✅ Operating principle: **piezoelectric effect** (Curie brothers, 1880)
- ✅ Material: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** is the industry standard
- ✅ Construction: housing → backing block → PZT → matching layer → lens
- ✅ Matching layer: **λ/4 thickness**, $Z_{match} = \sqrt{Z_{PZT} \cdot Z_{tissue}}$
- ✅ Resonant frequency (thickness mode): $f_0 = \frac{c_p}{2d}$
- ✅ Same crystal works as both **transmitter and receiver**
- ✅ **Damping block** = short pulse = better **axial resolution**
- ✅ Trade-off: **high frequency = better resolution but less penetration**

#### Must-Memorize Numerical Constants for KTU Exams
- $c_{soft\,tissue} = 1540$ m/s
- $\gamma_{hydrogen} = 42.58$ MHz/T
- $f_{Larmor} = 63.87$ MHz at 1.5 T
- HU of water = 0
- HU of air = −1000
- HU of fat = −100
- HU of cortical bone = +1000

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy for Module 4:**
> Always draw the **block diagram FIRST** (2 marks secured), then write stepwise **operation** (3 marks), then mention **clinical application / significance** (1–2 marks). Use **bold keywords** liberally — examiners scan for them. Always quote the **formulas** with the **correct units**. Practice drawing all four block diagrams (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, Transducer) in under 5 minutes each.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Therapeutic Devices - Pacemakers, Internal and External Pacemakers, Defibrillators, Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT721 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Therapeutic Devices - Pacemakers, Internal and External Pacemakers, Defibrillators, Basic Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cardiac Conduction System — Foundation of Therapeutic Devices

Before diving into pacemakers and defibrillators, a quick but essential recap of the heart's natural electrical system is required. The heart is a **muscular pump** whose contraction is initiated and coordinated by a specialized **electrical conduction system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Cardiac Conduction System (KTU 2024 Module 4 Prerequisite)**
> A specialized network of **autorhythmic (pacemaker) cardiac cells** that spontaneously generate and conduct electrical impulses, ensuring the **synchronized atrial and ventricular contraction** necessary to maintain adequate cardiac output.

### Order of Impulse Propagation

1. **SA Node (Sinoatrial Node)** — located in the right atrium; the **primary natural pacemaker** with an intrinsic rate of **60–100 bpm**.
2. **AV Node (Atrioventricular Node)** — delay station (~0.1 s) at the AV junction, allowing the atria to fully empty before ventricular contraction.
3. **Bundle of His** → **Right and Left Bundle Branches** → **Purkinje Fibres** — rapidly distribute the impulse to the ventricular myocardium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Significance:** When any component of this conduction system **fails (heart block), slows (bradycardia), or fires erratically (arrhythmia, fibrillation)**, an artificial **therapeutic device** (pacemaker or defibrillator) must intervene to restore a life-sustaining rhythm.

---

## 1.2 Therapeutic Devices — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Therapeutic Medical Devices (KTU 2024 Module 4)**
> Therapeutic devices in biomedical engineering refer to **electrical/electronic instruments designed to deliver controlled energy (electrical pulses, shocks, or radio-frequency currents) to a patient's body** in order to **diagnose, treat, or compensate for physiological malfunctions**, particularly in the cardiovascular and neuromuscular systems.

The two principal cardiovascular therapeutic devices studied in this module are:

| Device | Primary Clinical Function | Energy Delivered |
|---|---|---|
| **Pacemaker** | Restore or maintain adequate **heart rate** (bradycardia, heart block) | **Low-energy** repetitive pulses (µJ–mJ) |
| **Defibrillator** | Terminate **life-threatening tachyarrhythmias** (VF, pulseless VT) | **High-energy** synchronized or unsynchronized shock (Joules) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conductor and the Orchestra"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump 🎻 — The Heart as an Orchestra**
> Think of your heart as a **symphony orchestra**:
> - The **SA Node** is the **lead violinist** who sets the tempo. If absent or weak, the music drags → this is **bradycardia / heart block**, and a **pacemaker** steps in as a "metronome."
> - Sometimes, a few rogue musicians play out of sync (chaotic, fast, uncoordinated sounds) → this is **ventricular fibrillation**. The orchestra is silenced, and the **conductor fires a loud pistol shot (defibrillator)** to shock everyone into silence, after which the lead violinist (SA node) can resume control.

---

## 1.4 Key Physiological Constants to Memorize

- **Normal Sinus Rate:** **60–100 bpm**
- **Intrinsic Firing Rate of AV Node (junctional escape):** **40–60 bpm**
- **Ventricular Escape Rhythm:** **20–40 bpm** (often inadequate → requires pacing)
- **Internal Body Resistance (transthoracic):** **≈ 50 Ω (range 30–100 Ω)**
- **Threshold energy for defibrillation:** typically **200–360 J** (biphasic) / **360 J** (monophasic)
- **Pacemaker pulse amplitude:** typically **2.5–5 V** or **3–10 mA**, **0.4–0.5 ms** pulse width
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pacemakers — Engineering Principles

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pacemaker (KTU Board Definition)**
> A **pacemaker** is an implantable or external electronic device that delivers **periodic electrical pulses to the cardiac muscle** to stimulate contraction when the natural pacemaker (SA node) fails to maintain an adequate rate or rhythm.

### 2.1.2 Why Pacing is Needed — Clinical Indications

- **Sick Sinus Syndrome (SSS)** — SA node fires too slowly or irregularly.
- **Complete (Third-Degree) AV Block** — Atria and ventricles beat independently.
- **Symptomatic Bradycardia** (HR < 50 bpm with syncope/fatigue).
- **Post-cardiac-surgery temporary support.**
- **Heart failure** requiring **Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT)**.

### 2.1.3 Operational Principle — The "Threshold Concept"

A pacemaker delivers a **pulse of amplitude V and pulse width t** to the myocardium through an electrode. The pulse must exceed the **stimulation threshold** to depolarize the cardiac tissue.

$$E_{stimulus} = V \times I \times t$$

Where:
- $E$ = Energy delivered per pulse (Joules)
- $V$ = Pulse voltage (V)
- $I$ = Current (A)
- $t$ = Pulse duration (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strength-Duration Curve:** A hyperbolic relationship exists between the **minimum voltage** and the **minimum pulse duration** required to capture the heart. The **chronaxie time** is the pulse width at **twice the rheobase voltage** and is a critical design parameter (typically **0.4–0.5 ms** for endocardial pacing).

### 2.1.4 NBG Pacemaker Code (NASPE/BPEG Generic Code)

The **5-letter NBG code** is the international standard to describe pacing modes and is **mandatory KTU syllabus content**:

| Position | I | II | III | IV | V |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Chamber(s) Paced | Chamber(s) Sensed | Response to Sensing | Rate Modulation | Multisite Pacing |
| Common Codes | A, V, D | A, V, D | I, T, D | R | A, V, D |
| A=Atrium, V=Ventricle, D=Dual, I=Inhibited, T=Triggered, R=Rate-adaptive | | | | | |

**Example:** `DDDR` = Dual-chamber paced, Dual-chamber sensed, Dual response (inhibit + trigger), Rate-adaptive.

---

## 2.2 Types of Pacemakers — Internal vs External

### 2.2.1 Internal (Implantable) Pacemakers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Pacemaker (KTU Definition)**
> A **surgically implanted**, **battery-powered pulse generator** placed subcutaneously (usually in the **left pectoral region**), connected to the heart via **transvenous leads** (endocardial) that deliver chronic pacing therapy.

**Block Architecture:**

1. **Pulse Generator** (hermetically sealed titanium casing, weight ~25–50 g)
2. **Lithium-Iodine Battery** (typical life **5–12 years**, voltage **2.8 V**, capacity ~2 Ah)
3. **Microprocessor-based control circuit** (CMOS, with **read-only memory** storing pacing parameters)
4. **RF Telemetry Link** (for non-invasive programming)
5. **Lead(s)** — insulated conductors (often **bipolar** with cathode-anode 1 cm apart)
6. **Connector Header** (IS-1 standard since 1990s)

**Lead Types:**
- **Unipolar lead** — current flows between tip (cathode) and pulse generator can (anode). Larger pacing spike, more susceptible to myopotential sensing.
- **Bipolar lead** — both electrodes in the heart. Better signal fidelity, smaller artifact, preferred clinically.

### 2.2.2 External (Temporary) Pacemakers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Pacemaker (KTU Definition)**
> A **non-implantable** electronic device used for **short-term** pacing via either **transcutaneous** (skin-pad) or **transvenous** (catheter) electrodes. Used in **emergency, intra-operative, or bridge-to-permanent-pacemaker** scenarios.

**Two Sub-Types:**

| Feature | Transcutaneous (External) | Transvenous Temporary |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode placement | Skin pads (anterior & posterior chest) | Catheter via femoral/jugular vein to RV apex |
| Pulse amplitude | **20–200 mA** (high, due to skin resistance) | **1–10 mA** (direct endocardial contact) |
| Pulse width | **20–40 ms** | **0.4–2 ms** |
| Patient comfort | Painful — requires sedation | Tolerable under local anesthesia |
| Indication | **Emergency (code blue)** | ICU, post-op, awaiting permanent pacemaker |

### 2.2.3 Demand vs Fixed-Rate Pacemakers

- **Fixed-rate (Asynchronous)** — fires at preset rate regardless of intrinsic activity. Risk of **R-on-T phenomenon → VF**. Obsolete.
- **Demand (Synchronous)** — senses intrinsic QRS, **inhibits** output when natural rate is adequate. The modern standard.

---

## 2.3 Defibrillators — Engineering Principles

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defibrillator (KTU Board Definition)**
> A **defibrillator** is a therapeutic device that delivers a **brief, high-energy electrical shock** to the heart, with the purpose of **depolarizing a critical mass of the myocardium simultaneously**, thereby terminating a **re-entrant tachyarrhythmia** (ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia) and allowing the **SA node to resume normal sinus rhythm**.

### 2.3.2 Why a Shock Works — The "Critical Mass" Theory

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition 💡 — Why Defibrillation Saves Lives**
> In **ventricular fibrillation (VF)**, multiple re-entrant wavelets circulate chaotically, preventing coordinated contraction → no cardiac output → brain death in 4–6 minutes. A strong shock from a defibrillator **simultaneously depolarizes ~75% of the myocardium**, leaving the tissue in a **refractory state**, so all re-entrant circuits are extinguished. The **SA node (the heart's strongest autorhythmic focus)** then recaptures and re-establishes organized rhythm.

### 2.3.3 Fibrillation Threshold vs Defibrillation Threshold

$$DFT = k \times \sqrt{R}$$

Where:
- $DFT$ = Defibrillation Threshold (energy in Joules)
- $R$ = Transthoracic impedance (Ω)
- $k$ = Empirical constant

This indicates that **higher body impedance requires more energy**.

### 2.3.4 Energy and Charge Delivery — Capacitor Discharge Equation

The defibrillator's core is a **high-voltage capacitor (C)** charged to a high voltage and then discharged through the patient (resistance R) via paddles/pads.

The energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$$

Where:
- $E$ = Energy stored (Joules)
- $C$ = Capacitance (typically **32–45 µF**)
- $V$ = Charging voltage (typically up to **5000–7000 V DC**)

Discharge current through patient resistance $R$ as a function of time follows an **exponential decay**:

$$I(t) = \frac{V_0}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

The **peak current** is:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R}$$

### 2.3.5 Transthoracic Impedance

Adult transthoracic impedance is normally **30–100 Ω** (average ~50 Ω). The defibrillator must deliver current through this barrier. **Higher impedance** is seen in:
- Large chest size / obesity
- Poor paddle-skin contact
- Emphysema, pericardial effusion
- Paddle size too small

To reduce impedance, use **conductive gel pads** and **firm pressure (~25 lb on adult paddles)**.

### 2.3.6 Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveforms

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Current direction | Single polarity | Polarity reverses mid-shock |
| Energy (adult) | 200, 300, 360 J escalating | 120–200 J (often lower) |
| Efficacy | Lower first-shock success | **Higher** (~90% first-shock) |
| Myocardial injury | More | **Less** |
| Current KTU recommendation | Phasing out | **Gold standard** |

### 2.3.7 Types of Defibrillators

1. **Manual External Defibrillator** — clinician decides energy, charges, delivers.
2. **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** — analyzes rhythm, voice-prompted.
3. **Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD)** — implanted like a pacemaker, continuously monitors, auto-shocks VT/VF.
4. **Wearable Cardioverter Defibrillator (WCD)** — external vest for transient risk.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Variable | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V \times I \times t$ | Pacemaker pulse energy | Joules | Pulse parameter design |
| 2 | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Defibrillator stored energy | Joules | Energy calculation |
| 3 | $I_{peak} = V_0 / R$ | Peak discharge current | Ampere | Transthoracic analysis |
| 4 | $I(t) = (V_0/R) e^{-t/RC}$ | Discharge current vs time | Ampere | Waveform shape |
| 5 | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ | Voltage across patient | Volt | Exponential decay |
| 6 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of discharge | seconds | Pulse truncation design |
| 7 | $DFT \propto \sqrt{R}$ | Energy vs impedance | – | Energy escalation |
| 8 | $f_{stim} = 60 / T_{interval}$ | Pacing rate | bpm | Demand rate setting |
| 9 | $Charge = C \times V$ | Capacitor charge | Coulombs | Battery sizing |
| 10 | $t_{chronaxie} \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ ms | Chronaxie for endocardium | ms | Threshold curve |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When writing $CV^2/2$, examiners award full marks **only if the unit derivation is shown**. Always write $C$ in **farads** and $V$ in **volts** to yield joules.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pacemaker firmware** must detect **intrinsic QRS amplitudes as small as 2 mV** amid noise → drives **low-noise analog front-end design**.
- **Defibrillator design** balances **stored energy (bulky capacitor)** vs **portability** → encourages high-energy-density capacitors (e.g., 4500 V, 32 µF in <1 L).
- **Biphasic waveform** reduces myocardial stunning → led to the **implantable ICD revolution** (today >150,000 implanted/year in the US alone).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pacemaker Energy per Pulse

**Problem:** A demand pacemaker delivers pulses of amplitude **5 V** through an electrode-tissue load of **500 Ω**, with pulse width **0.5 ms**. Calculate the energy delivered per pulse and per hour (at 70 bpm).

**Step 1 — Compute the current through the load** using Ohm's law:

$$I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{500 \text{ }\Omega} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the power delivered** during the pulse:

$$P = V \times I = 5 \times 0.01 = 0.05 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy per pulse** by multiplying by pulse width:

$$E_{pulse} = P \times t = 0.05 \text{ W} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulses per hour** at 70 bpm:

$$N_{pulse} = 70 \text{ pulses/min} \times 60 \text{ min/h} = 4200 \text{ pulses/h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total energy per hour:**

$$E_{hour} = 4200 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 0.105 \text{ J/h}$$

**Step 6 — Compute battery life estimate.** A typical Li-I₂ cell stores ~2 Ah at 2.8 V → energy ~**20,160 J**. If pacing consumes ~0.1 J/h, the pacing circuitry alone would last >20,000 hours (~2.4 years) — *but* continuous telemetry and sensing reduce real life to **5–10 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why pacemaker batteries are **not rechargeable** but rather replaced whole — the pacing demand is too low to justify the added complexity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Defibrillator Discharge Waveform

**Problem:** A defibrillator charges a **32 µF capacitor** to **3000 V DC**. The patient represents a load of **50 Ω**. Compute the (a) stored energy, (b) peak current, and (c) time constant of discharge.

**Step (a) — Stored energy:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (3000)^2$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^{6} = \frac{1}{2} \times 288 = 144 \text{ J}$$

**Step (b) — Peak current** (at the instant of discharge, capacitor acts as voltage source):

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{3000}{50} = 60 \text{ A}$$

**Step (c) — Time constant:**

$$\tau = R \times C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step (d) — Voltage and current after 4 ms** (≈ 2.5 τ, current essentially decayed):

$$V(4\text{ ms}) = 3000 \cdot e^{-4/1.6} = 3000 \cdot e^{-2.5} = 3000 \times 0.0821 \approx 246 \text{ V}$$

$$I(4\text{ ms}) = 60 \cdot e^{-2.5} \approx 4.93 \text{ A}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight — Why pulse width matters:**
> Total discharge time of ~4 τ (~6.4 ms) defines the **monophasic pulse width**. Modern biphasic defibrillators **truncate** the first phase at ~3–5 ms, then reverse polarity for another 3–5 ms — the **Gurvich theorem** suggests this lowers DFT by ~30–40% for the same stored energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Charging Voltage for a Given Energy

**Problem:** A biphasic AED needs to deliver **150 J** to a 70 Ω patient. The capacitor is 200 µF. What voltage must the charger reach? (Assume 70% capacitor-to-patient transfer efficiency.)

**Step 1 — Required stored energy on capacitor:**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{E_{delivered}}{\eta} = \frac{150}{0.70} \approx 214.3 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for V from E = ½CV²:**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{stored}}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 214.3}{200 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{2{,}143{,}000} = 1464 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the delivered energy:**

$$E_{delivered} = \eta \times \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = 0.7 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times (1464)^2 \approx 150 \text{ J} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real AED charging circuit:** A flyback DC-DC converter steps a 12 V battery to ~1500 V at ~5 W average power. Charging 200 µF to 1500 V in ~6 seconds is achievable — this is why AEDs run on consumer lithium batteries.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pacemaker vs Defibrillator

| Parameter | Pacemaker | Defibrillator |
|---|---|---|
| Energy range | **µJ to mJ per pulse** | **50–360 J per shock** |
| Pulse width | 0.2–2 ms | 3–12 ms (biphasic phase) |
| Voltage delivered | 2.5–10 V | 1000–5000 V |
| Current | 3–15 mA | 30–100 A |
| Frequency | Continuous (~60–100 ppm) | One-shot, repeated as needed |
| Sensing required | Yes (QRS detection) | Yes (rhythm analysis) |
| Synchronization | Demand mode (inhibit) | Cardioversion (sync); Defibrillation (async) |
| Implantation | Subcutaneous pocket | External / ICD in chest |
| Battery | Li-I₂ (low current) | Li-MnO₂ or Li-CFx (high current pulses) |
| KTU code | NBG: AAIR, VVIR, DDDR | – |

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table — Internal vs External Pacemaker

| Feature | Internal (Implantable) | External (Temporary) |
|---|---|---|
| Placement | Subcutaneous (left pectoral) | Beside bed / handheld |
| Electrode | Transvenous endocardial (screw-in or tined) | Transcutaneous pads OR transvenous catheter |
| Pulse amplitude | 2.5–5 V | 20–200 mA (TC) / 1–10 mA (TV) |
| Pulse width | 0.4–0.5 ms | 20–40 ms (TC) / 0.4–2 ms (TV) |
| Battery | Li-I₂ internal, 5–12 yr | AC mains / rechargeable |
| Indication | Chronic AV block, SSS | Emergency, post-op, bridge |
| Patient discomfort | None | Painful (transcutaneous) |
| KTU exam weight | **High** (5–8 marks) | Moderate (3–5 marks) |
