# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a pacemaker. List the components of an implantable pacemaker with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pacemaker** is an electronic device that delivers periodic low-energy electrical pulses to the heart muscle to stimulate contraction when the natural SA node fails to maintain adequate rate/rhythm. **(1 mark)**

**Components of an implantable pacemaker:**

1. **Pulse generator** — sealed titanium case containing CMOS circuitry. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Lithium-iodine battery** — 2.8 V, life 5–12 years. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Endocardial lead(s)** — bipolar, with tip cathode. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Connector header** (IS-1) and **RF telemetry coil** for programming. **(0.25 mark)**
5. **Microprocessor** for demand mode and rate adaptation. **(0.25 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **RF telemetry/programmer link**. This is a common 0.5-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a defibrillator? Explain the basic principle of defibrillation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **defibrillator** is a therapeutic device that delivers a brief, high-energy electrical shock across the heart to terminate life-threatening arrhythmias such as **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** and **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT)**. **(1 mark)**

**Basic principle:** When VF occurs, multiple **re-entrant wavelets** circulate chaotically in the myocardium. A high-energy shock (e.g., 150–360 J) **simultaneously depolarizes a critical mass (~75%) of the myocardial cells**, leaving them in a refractory state. This **extinguishes the re-entrant circuits**, allowing the **SA node** — the heart's natural autorhythmic focus — to **recapture and re-establish organized sinus rhythm**. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Choice A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **implantable (internal) pacemaker**. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **need for pacing** and differentiate clearly between **fixed-rate (asynchronous) and demand (synchronous)** pacemakers. Why is the demand mode clinically preferred? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — Implantable Pacemaker Block Diagram & Working (7 marks)

A modern **implantable pacemaker** is a hermetically sealed, battery-powered device placed subcutaneously in the left pectoral region, connected to the heart via transvenous leads.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Battery → DC-DC Converter → Output Capacitor → CMOS Logic → HV Switch → Lead
             ↑                                       ↓
        Programmer ← RF Telemetry ← Sensed ECG
```

**Working of Each Block (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Battery (Lithium-Iodine):** Provides 2.8 V, ~2 Ah, ensuring 5–12 years of life. Chemically stable, no gas generation.
2. **DC-DC Converter:** Boosts 2.8 V to a stable, low-impedance supply for the pacing pulse (e.g., 5 V).
3. **ECG Sensing Front-End:** Detects intrinsic QRS complexes via the same lead (bandwidth 20–200 Hz, sensitivity 2 mV).
4. **CMOS Microcontroller Logic:** Compares sensed rate with programmed lower rate limit. If intrinsic rate > set rate → **inhibits** output. If lower → schedules a pacing pulse.
5. **HV Switch (MOSFET):** Connects the output capacitor to the lead for ~0.4–0.5 ms, delivering a programmable 2.5–5 V pulse.
6. **RF Telemetry & Programmer:** Allows the cardiologist to **non-invasively adjust** rate, amplitude, pulse width, AV delay, etc.

**Refractory period** (~300 ms) is built in to prevent T-wave sensing → unwanted inhibition. **(1 mark for refractory mention)**

**Unipolar vs Bipolar leads** — modern pacemakers default to bipolar to reduce myopotential oversensing and muscle stimulation. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — Need for Pacing & Demand vs Fixed-Rate (7 marks)

**Need for Pacing (3 marks):**

- **Sick Sinus Syndrome** — SA node fails, heart rate drops below 50 bpm, leading to syncope, fatigue, and reduced cardiac output.
- **Complete (3rd degree) AV Block** — Atrial and ventricular activity decoupled, ventricular escape rhythm (20–40 bpm) is insufficient.
- **Post-MI bradycardia** due to ischemia of conduction tissue.
- **Symptomatic bradycardia** of any cause (drug-induced, neurocardiogenic, post-cardiac surgery).

**Fixed-Rate (Asynchronous) Pacemaker (2 marks):**

- Fires at a preset rate (e.g., 70 ppm) **regardless** of intrinsic cardiac activity.
- **Major risk: R-on-T phenomenon** — if a pacing pulse lands on the T-wave (vulnerable period), it can trigger **ventricular fibrillation**.
- No sensing circuitry needed → simple and cheap, but clinically obsolete.

**Demand (Synchronous) Pacemaker (2 marks):**

- Continuously **senses intrinsic QRS complexes** through the lead.
- If the natural rate exceeds the programmed lower rate limit, output is **inhibited** (VVI mode: Ventricular-paced, Ventricular-sensed, Inhibited response).
- Avoids competition with intrinsic rhythm → **eliminates R-on-T risk**.
- Modern pacemakers also offer **DDD (dual-chamber, dual-sense, dual-response)** and **rate-adaptive (R) modes** for physiological pacing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Not stating the rate range** (e.g., 60–100 bpm normal, <50 bradycardic) — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing VVI with VOO** — VVI = demand, VOO = asynchronous. The third letter (I/T/D) is critical and frequently miswritten.
> 3. **Forgetting R-on-T phenomenon** when explaining why demand mode is preferred — biggest single mark-loss point.
> 4. **Failing to mention refractory period** in part (a) — examiners specifically look for this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the **basic principle of a DC defibrillator** with a neat block diagram. Derive the energy stored in the capacitor and explain the discharge current waveform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **monophasic and biphasic defibrillators**. Discuss **implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs)** and their clinical use. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — DC Defibrillator Principle (7 marks)

**Basic Principle (2 marks):**

A DC defibrillator converts AC mains (or battery) into a high-voltage DC charge, stores this energy in a large capacitor (32–45 µF charged to 1500–5000 V), and then rapidly discharges it through the patient's thorax via two paddle electrodes. The shock, typically **150–360 J**, depolarizes a critical mass of the myocardium, terminating VF.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
AC Mains → Step-up Transformer → HV Rectifier → Charging Resistor → Capacitor (32 µF)
                                                                            ↓
                                                              HV Switch (Triggered)
                                                                            ↓
                                                                Patient (50 Ω) via Paddles
```

**Derivation — Stored Energy (2 marks):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$$

For $C = 32 \times 10^{-6}$ F and $V = 4000$ V:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (4000)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 16 \times 10^6 = 256 \text{ J}$$

**Discharge Waveform (1 mark):**

$$I(t) = \frac{V_0}{R}e^{-t/RC}$$

For R = 50 Ω, C = 32 µF: $\tau = RC = 1.6$ ms. The current decays exponentially; modern defibrillators **truncate** the pulse at ~3–5 ms and may reverse polarity (biphasic).

---

#### Part (b) — Monophasic vs Biphasic & ICDs (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Current path | One direction (anode → cathode) | Reverses mid-shock |
| Peak current | Higher for same energy | Lower (more efficient) |
| Energy needed | 200–360 J | 120–200 J |
| DFT | Higher | Lower (~30–40% reduction) |
| Myocardial injury | Greater ST-segment changes | Reduced stunning |
| First-shock success | ~60% | ~90% |
| KTU recommendation | Phased out | **Standard of care** |

**Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD) (4 marks):**

- **Definition:** A subcutaneously implanted device (~40 g) that continuously monitors the heart rhythm, automatically detects VT/VF, and delivers **anti-tachycardia pacing (ATP)** or **defibrillation shocks (10–35 J)** internally.
- **Components:** Hermetic titanium can, Li-MnO₂ battery (life 5–9 years), capacitor (high-energy-density), sensing leads, RV/SVC shocking coils.
- **Therapies delivered in tiers:**
  1. **ATP** — bursts of rapid pacing to terminate VT painlessly.
  2. **Cardioversion** — low-energy synchronized shock for VT.
  3. **Defibrillation** — high-energy shock (10–35 J internal) for VF.
- **Indications:** Survivors of sudden cardiac arrest, LVEF <35% post-MI, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, long-QT syndrome.
- **Advantages over external:** 24/7 monitoring, immediate therapy, no need for external rescuer.
- **Disadvantages:** Invasive, expensive, risk of inappropriate shocks, generator replacement every 5–9 years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B:**
> 1. **Writing $CV$ instead of $CV^2$** in the energy derivation — the **square** is non-negotiable. Lose 1 full mark if missed.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** for $C$ (F) and $V$ (V) in the derivation — examiner awards 0.5 mark only if units are consistent.
> 3. **Not mentioning anti-tachycardia pacing (ATP)** in ICD therapy — this is a frequently-asked 2-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Failing to differentiate cardioversion (synchronized) from defibrillation (unsynchronized)** — both are shocks, but the synchronization is a board favorite.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Therapeutic Devices)**

### Pacemaker Essentials
- ✅ Definition: low-energy device to **stimulate** cardiac contraction.
- ✅ **Internal** = implanted, transvenous lead, 2.5–5 V, 0.4–0.5 ms, 5–12 yr Li-I₂ battery.
- ✅ **External** = temporary, transcutaneous (20–200 mA, 20–40 ms) or transvenous (1–10 mA, 0.4–2 ms).
- ✅ **NBG code** is 5-letter: Chamber paced / Chamber sensed / Response / Rate modulation / Multisite.
- ✅ **VVI** = ventricular demand; **DDD** = dual-chamber dual-response; **AAIR** = atrial rate-adaptive.
- ✅ **Demand mode** preferred over fixed-rate to avoid **R-on-T → VF**.
- ✅ **Chronaxie** ≈ 0.4–0.5 ms for endocardial pacing.

### Defibrillator Essentials
- ✅ Definition: high-energy device to **terminate re-entrant tachyarrhythmias** (VF/pVT).
- ✅ Stored energy: $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ (C = 32–200 µF, V up to 5000 V).
- ✅ Discharge waveform: $I(t) = (V_0/R) e^{-t/RC}$.
- ✅ Transthoracic impedance: ~50 Ω, range 30–100 Ω.
- ✅ **Monophasic** → **Biphasic** is the modern gold standard.
- ✅ **Cardioversion** = synchronized (R-wave triggered); **Defibrillation** = unsynchronized.
- ✅ **ICD** = implantable, automatic VT/VF therapy (ATP → cardioversion → defibrillation).
- ✅ **AED** = automated external, rhythm analysis + voice prompt.

### Engineering Constants to Memorize
- ✅ Normal sinus rate: **60–100 bpm**
- ✅ Pacing energy: **µJ–mJ per pulse**
- ✅ Defibrillation energy: **150–360 J**
- ✅ Pacemaker battery: **2.8 V Li-I₂, 2 Ah, 5–12 yr**
- ✅ Defibrillator capacitor: **32–45 µF, 1500–5000 V**
- ✅ Transthoracic impedance: **~50 Ω**
- ✅ Chronaxie: **0.4–0.5 ms**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** In any exam question on pacemakers or defibrillators, **always start with a labeled block diagram** (minimum 1–2 marks guaranteed), then derive any numerical answer step-by-step with units, and end with a clinical correlate. This presentation style is what KTU examiners reward.
